EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the mid-Atlantic late on September 8. Widespread showers and thunderstorms developed in a warm and humid air mass ahead of the front, moving into eastern Pennsylvania during the evening and early overnight hours. With instability waning after dark, much of the convection remained sub-severe. However, some instances of damaging wind did occur, particularly along a bowing line segment in Berks County. Showers and storms continued to gradually weaken as they shifted east later in the night.

A weak cold front dissipated west of the mid-Atlantic on June 4. However, a lingering surface trough slowly moved through the region. With a lingering warm and moist air mass in place, the surface trough caused convection to develop, starting early in the afternoon. Despite weak wind shear, storms steadily increased in organization, aided by favorable mesoscale interactions. Several storms would go on to pose a threat for damaging winds and hail. Storms eventually coalesced into multiple linear segments which posed a continued wind damage threat. Storms moved offshore by early to mid evening, with a drier air mass behind them.

A warm front moved through the mid-Atlantic on the morning of June 3. Considerable cloud cover lingered during and after the front passage, but breaks of sunshine gradually developed by afternoon. This, combined with a surge of moisture behind the warm front, allowed a moderate amount of instability to develop. A pre-frontal surface trough served as a focus for convective development by late afternoon. Convection became fairly widespread, but the moderate instability combined with only weak to moderate shear limited its overall severity, and the high moisture contributed to a messy convective evolution. A few reports of damaging wind were received, and storms also produced locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed near Armorcrast Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An advancing cold front, along with shortwave support, created the chance for thunderstorms across the region. Sufficient shear (40kts in the 0-6km layer), moderate to high instability (1500-2000 J/kg of CAPE), and dry mid-level air resulted in damaging winds and hail. Flash flooding occurred, mostly south of Pittsburgh, with training storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter sized hail reported on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front approached the mid-Atlantic coast on April 21. Ahead of the front, modest instability developed in a strongly sheared and strongly forced environment. This led to the development of convection along and ahead of the front as it moved through during the mid afternoon hours. Mixed modes of linear and discrete storm cells were present, some of which became strong to severe. Scattered instances of hail and wind damage were reported across much of the eastern mid-Atlantic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Ida impacted the mid-Atlantic on September 1, 2021. As it moved northward, Ida began transitioning to a strong mid-latitude cyclone with pronounced frontal features, but with continued tropical moisture. On a regional level, the remnants of Ida became one of the most severe natural disasters in US history, due to a combination of several tornadoes, some of them strong, as well as catastrophic flooding over a large and densely populated area. The severe weather threat unfolded as the system's developing warm front lifted northward from the Delmarva region into Pennsylvania and New Jersey. South of the warm front, partial clearing and moderate to strong instability developed. Meanwhile, very strong shear and forcing, along with tropical moisture, were present within the warm sector. The result was widespread convection in the form of both supercell and quasi-linear thunderstorms. A number of storms became severe across the region, producing several tornadoes and other instances of wind damage. This continued an exceptionally active stretch of high end severe weather in the region, coming barely a month after the tornado outbreak of July 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The storm which produced a strong tornado in Montgomery County, |PA, cycled and produced another tornado in Bucks County, PA. |Damage began near in the area of Neshaminy Creek between Valley Rd|and Pebble Hill Rd in Doylestown. The tornado tracked north- |northeast from there, producing a consistent path of damage. |Damage was mainly confined to trees and was fairly extensive at |times, with much of the path length being heavily wooded. Numerous|hardwood and softwood trees were snapped and uprooted along the |path. Damage was strongly suggestive of tornadic winds, and a |tornado debris signature was also evident on radar. The heaviest |damage occurred near and along a stretch of Edison- Furlong Rd. |Extensive tree damage occurred along that road and a number of its|cross streets. Several utility poles were snapped in this area. |Tree damage along nearby Swamp Rd was also significant. The |tornado continued but began to lose intensity as it approached |Route 202, with sporadic tree damage observed on Mill Rd. Gradual |weakening continued and the tornado appeared to lift entirely in |the area of Burnt House Hill Rd. The extent of tree damage |suggested maximum winds of around 100 mph. No injuries were |reported as a result of this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure in the Gulf of Maine dragged a strong cold front across central Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of April 30, 2021. A secondary cold front crossed the area during the day on April 30. These systems produced strong winds across the area, particularly across southeastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were estimated to have gusted to near 60 mph in Dauphin County on April 30, 2021. This wind event produced numerous downed trees and power lines in Dauphin County, particularly in the central and southern parts of the county. A 62 mph gust was measured by the ASOS at Harrisburg International Airport.

One storm in southeast Pennsylvania briefly became severe, before activity continued to weaken as it moved off to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shingles were blown off a roof and minor tree damage occurred in Upper Chichester Twp. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Post Tropical Cyclone Ida brought heavy rain to eastern Pennsylvania on September 1.  Rainfall totals were as high as 5 to 10 inches.  The heavy rain caused significant flash flooding, mainly in the southeastern part of the state.  It resulted in widespread property damage.  There were several fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warner Road was closed at PA Route 715 in Pocono Township due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Elsa approached the mid-Atlantic states from the south on July 8. Downstream of Elsa, a warm and moist environment was present. A slow moving frontal boundary also resided over northern New Jersey, northern Pennsylvania, and into New York. This frontal system provided forcing for convective development over the mid-Atlantic, in fairly close proximity but unrelated to Tropical Storm Elsa. With strong instability and weak to moderate wind shear, some of the storms became severe as they organized into linear and multi-cellular clusters. Deep layer southerly flow in advance of Elsa caused storms to move northward as opposed to a more typical eastward motion. Damaging wind was the main hazard associated with these storms, with several strong downbursts noted. Storm activity eventually weakened and moved north of the region by evening as Elsa moved in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video showed multiple large trees down on power lines along Almshouse Rd in Doylestown. Time estimated from radar.

Hot and humid conditions existed over the mid-Atlantic on July 6, with a cold front well northwest over the Great Lakes and southern Canada. As temperatures rose into the 90s with dew points in the upper 60s and low 70s, strong instability built over the region. A pre-frontal trough and a fast moving upper level disturbance provided some lift, which helped convection develop by mid afternoon. Convection was initially concentrated over northern Pennsylvania and New York, near the better forcing, but propagated southeastward with time. Shear was weak to moderate but sufficient to allow some storm organization into multi-cellular and linear segments. A number of storms became severe, producing damaging winds and some hail. As storms moved southeast, outflow boundaries moving even further south helped ignite additional convection, some severe, which continued into the early overnight hours. The early overnight convection was also noted to be especially prolific with regard to lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell and other branches and limbs were downed in the area from a likely downburst. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Elsa approached the mid-Atlantic states from the south on July 8. Downstream of Elsa, a warm and moist environment was present. A slow moving frontal boundary also resided over northern New Jersey, northern Pennsylvania, and into New York. This frontal system provided forcing for convective development over the mid-Atlantic, in fairly close proximity but unrelated to Tropical Storm Elsa. With strong instability and weak to moderate wind shear, some of the storms became severe as they organized into linear and multi-cellular clusters. Deep layer southerly flow in advance of Elsa caused storms to move northward as opposed to a more typical eastward motion. Damaging wind was the main hazard associated with these storms, with several strong downbursts noted. Storm activity eventually weakened and moved north of the region by evening as Elsa moved in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto a house on Cedar Rd. No injuries occurred but the home was rendered uninhabitable. Time estimated from radar and emergency management reports.

Low pressure was slowly moving northeast from IN through northern OH during the day of July 11th, 2021, lifting a warm front northward towards northwest PA. Showers and thunderstorms moved northeast across Erie County, PA for much of the daytime hours ahead of this front. An low echo centroid storm with intense rainfall rates around 1.5 per hour produce efficient heavy rainfall over an area with saturated soils from the showers during the morning and afternoon hours. PWAT values were near 1.8, with modest MUCAPE values generally 500 j/kg or less. This intense burst of rain dropped around 1.5 in a little over an hour in the Edinboro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported several road closures in and around Edinboro due to flooding. Reports of numerous flooded basements. Received video of a creek out of it's banks, with several inches of water flowing over Gibson Hill Road near Capp Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the northern Gulf of Mexico on the morning of February 15 and moved northeast into the Tennessee and Ohio River Valleys through the afternoon and evening hours. Well ahead of this low pressure system, a band of snow overspread the region with a weak front during the morning hours of the 15th, allowing for a quick inch or two of snow across the area. As the low crept closer to the region, deepening over the Ohio/Pennsylvania border and bringing ample Gulf moisture into the region, a broader area of precipitation moved through the area during the afternoon and evening hours. For Northwest Pennsylvania, this precipitation started in the form of sno, but by evening, a nose of warmer air just above the surface allowed for snow to melt and for the end precipitation type to become a mix of sleet and freezing rain significantly lessening snowfall amounts in several locations. Behind the low pressure system, colder air surged into the region and lake effect snow developed over Lake Erie and entered Northeast Ohio on the 16th. By the late evening of the 16th, an additional 4 to 8 inches of snow fell in portions of the snow belt, adding to the wintry mix from the night before. Impacts were mixed in Northwest Pennsylvania as the first half of the event underperformed but the lake effect on the 16th overperformed, leading to mixed preparedness for the region. For the entire event, the highest total snowfall measurement in Pennsylvania was 11 inches by a trained snow spotter northwest of Edinboro in south central Erie County. Erie International Airport only recorded 2.8 inches of snow for this event. There were no known injuries or fatalities in Northwest Pennsylvania on account of this winter storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three rounds of snow moved through Northwest Pennsylvania on February 15 and 16. The first round on the morning of the 15th brought a quick inch of snow to the region. A second round of snow entered during the afternoon and evening hours, bringing an additional 1 to 4 inches of snow to the region. For a brief period during the evening of the 15th, snow transitioned to sleet and freezing rain, as some warmer air aloft melted the snow but near surface temperatures below freezing allowed for wintry precipitation to continue. Sleet amounts of a few tenths and ice amounts up to one tenth were observed across the area. A third round of snow came in the form of lake effect snow and an additional 1 to 3 inches of snow fell. Total snowfall for the event for the northern portions of Erie County ranged from roughly 3 to 9 inches of snow. The maximum snowfall for this event was in Spring Township, where a trained snow spotter measured 10 inches of snow for the event. Other selected snowfall totals for the event include: 6 inches in Meadville, 5 inches in Springboro, 4 inches northwest of Jamestown, and 3.6 inches in Atlantic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, unstable airmass has been in place over the region for much of the week leading up to July 17th, 2021, with several rounds of rainfall during the week leading to saturated soils across northwest PA. Thunderstorms developed ahead of an eastward moving cold front, with low echo centroid storms providing efficient, heavy rainfall. Two storms tracked over far southeast Crawford county in PA, dropping over 2 of rainfall in a one to two hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Titusville Fire Department reported significant flash flooding in Titusville due to Church Run, and additional flooding in Hydetown due to Oil Creek. The majority of flooding reports were from downtown Titusville, where video shows rapidly flowing water through the streets up against buildings and businesses. One water rescue was reported, with several reports of flooded basements. Numerous roads were closed, with Franklin Street being the main road impacted by flowing water. Rainfall amounts of 2 to 2.5 fell just upstream of the city of Titusville in a one to two hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A near record warm air mass was in place over the mid-Atlantic on June 30. Combined with fairly high humidity, moderate to strong instability built over the region. An approaching surface trough acted to trigger scattered thunderstorms over the area during the afternoon and evening hours. Shear was not very strong, which limited overall storm organization. However, some storms did become strong to severe and produced instances of damaging winds. Convective activity diminished during the later evening hours with the loss of daytime heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed near Old New Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm produced widespread snowfall across central Pennsylvania from January 31 to February 2, 2021. Although the snow began on January 31st, warning criteria was not met in any central Pennsylvania counties until February 1st or 2nd. Widespread snowfall amounts of 10-15 inches were observed, with more than 20 inches of accumulation in portions of Schuylkill County. This was a Miller Type B storm, with the primarily low pushing up the Ohio Valley before a center-jump to the Mid-Atlantic coast was observed. Snow was the predominant precipitation type, although the Lower Susquehanna Valley had a several hour period of light wintry mix before the coastal low strengthened. The system then very slowly pulled east-northeastward along the southern New England coast and out to sea.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 9 to 11 inches of snow across northern Lycoming County from January 31 to February 2, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a more than two week stretch of dry weather, a cold front approached the mid-Atlantic on May 26. Ahead of the front, an unseasonably hot and humid air mass developed, with high temperatures near to above 90 degrees in much of the region. This contributed to strong instability, while wind shear was more moderate. Multiple rounds of convection affected the region, triggered by a pre-frontal trough and a couple of upper level impulses ahead of the front. A fairly widespread severe weather event resulted, with damaging winds by far the most widespread hazard. Numerous instances of downed trees and power lines and some property damage were reported. Thunderstorms during the evening hours were also prolific lightning producers. The final round of storms finally moved offshore close to midnight after several hours of severe weather in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed near Sixth St and Market St. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture-laden system developed over the Gulf States and pushes up the spine of the Appalachians, bring a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain to central Pennsylvania from the evening of February 15 into the morning of February 16. The Lower Susquehanna Valley quickly went over to plain rain after a brief period of freezing rain, while the central mountains had a prolonged period of sleet and freezing rain with many locations receiving over 0.25 of ice accretion. North of I-80, there was 1-2 inches of snow and sleet followed by freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced a quarter of an inch of ice accumulation, plus half an inch of sleet and 2 inches of snow to Elk County from February 15-16, 2021.

North of I-80, there was 1-2 inches of snow and sleet followed by freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced a quarter of an inch of ice accumulation, plus half an inch of sleet and 1 to 2 inches of snow to Cameron County from February 15-16, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm produced widespread snowfall across central Pennsylvania from January 31 to February 2, 2021. Although the snow began on January 31st, warning criteria was not met in any central Pennsylvania counties until February 1st or 2nd. Widespread snowfall amounts of 10-15 inches were observed, with more than 20 inches of accumulation in portions of Schuylkill County. This was a Miller Type B storm, with the primarily low pushing up the Ohio Valley before a center-jump to the Mid-Atlantic coast was observed. Snow was the predominant precipitation type, although the Lower Susquehanna Valley had a several hour period of light wintry mix before the coastal low strengthened. The system then very slowly pulled east-northeastward along the southern New England coast and out to sea.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 14 to 21 inches of snow across Schuylkill County from January 31 to February 2, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a more than two week stretch of dry weather, a cold front approached the mid-Atlantic on May 26. Ahead of the front, an unseasonably hot and humid air mass developed, with high temperatures near to above 90 degrees in much of the region. This contributed to strong instability, while wind shear was more moderate. Multiple rounds of convection affected the region, triggered by a pre-frontal trough and a couple of upper level impulses ahead of the front. A fairly widespread severe weather event resulted, with damaging winds by far the most widespread hazard. Numerous instances of downed trees and power lines and some property damage were reported. Thunderstorms during the evening hours were also prolific lightning producers. The final round of storms finally moved offshore close to midnight after several hours of severe weather in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Time of event estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain associated with Post Tropical Cyclone Ida produced moderate to major flooding along the tidal Delaware River on the night of September 1.  There was another round of moderate flooding on the night of September 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred along the tidal Delaware River and its tidal tributaries in lower Bucks County, inundating adjacent roadways and properties.  The tide gauge at Newbold, Pennsylvania reached a level of 11.51 feet Mean Lower Low Water.  The tide gauge at Burlington, New Jersey reached a level of 10.41 feet Mean Lower Low Water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of January 31st, weakening low pressure moved northward into the lower Ohio Valley. Frontogenetic forcing allowed for some higher snow rates during the morning and early afternoon hours, particularly in the higher elevations. Snow rates became lighter in the afternoon as support for lift weakened and lifted northward, and as energy began to transfer to a coastal low. Accumulations through midnight were generally limited to four inches or less. ||This event continued into early February, when strengthening northwest flow increased cold advection and allowed additional accumulating snow showers to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highest storm total snowfall from January 31st through February 2nd was 9.5 inches in Bethel Park.

Widespread showers and thunderstorms developed in a warm and humid air mass ahead of the front, moving into eastern Pennsylvania during the evening and early overnight hours. With instability waning after dark, much of the convection remained sub-severe. However, some instances of damaging wind did occur, particularly along a bowing line segment in Berks County. Showers and storms continued to gradually weaken as they shifted east later in the night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed along Locust Ridge Rd near Arrowhead Lake. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm produced widespread snowfall across central Pennsylvania from January 31 to February 2, 2021. Although the snow began on January 31st, warning criteria was not met in any central Pennsylvania counties until February 1st or 2nd. Widespread snowfall amounts of 10-15 inches were observed, with more than 20 inches of accumulation in portions of Schuylkill County. This was a Miller Type B storm, with the primarily low pushing up the Ohio Valley before a center-jump to the Mid-Atlantic coast was observed. Snow was the predominant precipitation type, although the Lower Susquehanna Valley had a several hour period of light wintry mix before the coastal low strengthened. The system then very slowly pulled east-northeastward along the southern New England coast and out to sea.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 8 to 12 inches of snow across York County from January 31 to February 2, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a more than two week stretch of dry weather, a cold front approached the mid-Atlantic on May 26. Ahead of the front, an unseasonably hot and humid air mass developed, with high temperatures near to above 90 degrees in much of the region. This contributed to strong instability, while wind shear was more moderate. Multiple rounds of convection affected the region, triggered by a pre-frontal trough and a couple of upper level impulses ahead of the front. A fairly widespread severe weather event resulted, with damaging winds by far the most widespread hazard. Numerous instances of downed trees and power lines and some property damage were reported. Thunderstorms during the evening hours were also prolific lightning producers. The final round of storms finally moved offshore close to midnight after several hours of severe weather in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs and wires were downed near Westwood Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of January 31st, weakening low pressure moved northward into the lower Ohio Valley. Frontogenetic forcing allowed for some higher snow rates during the morning and early afternoon hours, particularly in the higher elevations. Snow rates became lighter in the afternoon as support for lift weakened and lifted northward, and as energy began to transfer to a coastal low. Accumulations through midnight were generally limited to four inches or less. ||This event continued into early February, when strengthening northwest flow increased cold advection and allowed additional accumulating snow showers to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highest storm total snowfall from Jan 31st-Feb 3rd was 6.5 inches near Dayton, PA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture-laden system developed over the Gulf States and pushes up the spine of the Appalachians, bring a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain to central Pennsylvania from the evening of February 15 into the morning of February 16. The Lower Susquehanna Valley quickly went over to plain rain after a brief period of freezing rain, while the central mountains had a prolonged period of sleet and freezing rain with many locations receiving over 0.25 of ice accretion. North of I-80, there was 1-2 inches of snow and sleet followed by freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice storm produced 0.25 inches of ice accumulation across Snyder County from February 15-16, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed over the Great Lakes and up the Saint Lawrence River valley from February 22 into February 23, 2021, pushing a warm front and cold front across central Pennsylvania. A narrow, north-south stripe of heavy snow (1-2 per hour) associated with the warm front passed over the Susquehanna Valley during the morning rush hour, and the heavy snowfall rates combined with the potential for a flash freeze prompted the issuance of snow squall warnings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall on the leading edge of a mesoscale band of heavy snow briefly dropped visibility to under one quarter mile in and produced flash-freeze conditions in Northumberland County.

Frontogenetic forcing allowed for some higher snow rates during the morning and early afternoon hours, particularly in the higher elevations. Snow rates became lighter in the afternoon as support for lift weakened and lifted northward, and as energy began to transfer to a coastal low. Accumulations through midnight were generally limited to four inches or less. ||This event continued into early February, when strengthening northwest flow increased cold advection and allowed additional accumulating snow showers to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An average of 6 inches fell in Westmoreland County from Jan 31st to Feb 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a more than two week stretch of dry weather, a cold front approached the mid-Atlantic on May 26. Ahead of the front, an unseasonably hot and humid air mass developed, with high temperatures near to above 90 degrees in much of the region. This contributed to strong instability, while wind shear was more moderate. Multiple rounds of convection affected the region, triggered by a pre-frontal trough and a couple of upper level impulses ahead of the front. A fairly widespread severe weather event resulted, with damaging winds by far the most widespread hazard. Numerous instances of downed trees and power lines and some property damage were reported. Thunderstorms during the evening hours were also prolific lightning producers. The final round of storms finally moved offshore close to midnight after several hours of severe weather in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An mPing report of downed tree limbs and power lines near Old Topton Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed in the lower TN Valley and moved northeast along the western side of the Appalachian Range. A strong band of heavy snow began to develop along the northwest side of the low as it tracked northeast. The lift and moisture available was enough to create an area of intensification along the front which resulted in some areas receiving around 2 to 3 inches per hour snowfall rates. As a result, some locations in Ohio had over achieving snowfall amounts that went over 6 inches in some instances.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of 6 to 6.5 inches of snow in portions of Allegheny County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm produced widespread snowfall across central Pennsylvania from January 31 to February 2, 2021. Although the snow began on January 31st, warning criteria was not met in any central Pennsylvania counties until February 1st or 2nd. Widespread snowfall amounts of 10-15 inches were observed, with more than 20 inches of accumulation in portions of Schuylkill County. This was a Miller Type B storm, with the primarily low pushing up the Ohio Valley before a center-jump to the Mid-Atlantic coast was observed. Snow was the predominant precipitation type, although the Lower Susquehanna Valley had a several hour period of light wintry mix before the coastal low strengthened. The system then very slowly pulled east-northeastward along the southern New England coast and out to sea.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 8 to 12 inches of snow across Adams County from January 31 to February 2, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of January 31st, weakening low pressure moved northward into the lower Ohio Valley. Frontogenetic forcing allowed for some higher snow rates during the morning and early afternoon hours, particularly in the higher elevations. Snow rates became lighter in the afternoon as support for lift weakened and lifted northward, and as energy began to transfer to a coastal low. Accumulations through midnight were generally limited to four inches or less. ||This event continued into early February, when strengthening northwest flow increased cold advection and allowed additional accumulating snow showers to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Average storm total snowfall in the ridges of Westmoreland County from Jan 31st to Feb 3rd was 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain associated with Post Tropical Cyclone Ida produced moderate to major flooding along the tidal Delaware River on the night of September 1.  There was another round of moderate flooding on the night of September 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding occurred along the tidal Delaware River and its tidal tributaries in lower Bucks County, inundating adjacent roadways and properties.  The tide gauge at Newbold, Pennsylvania reached a record level of 13.22 feet Mean Lower Low Water.  The tide gauge at Burlington, New Jersey tied its record level of 11.94 feet Mean Lower Low Water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm produced widespread snowfall across central Pennsylvania from January 31 to February 2, 2021. Although the snow began on January 31st, warning criteria was not met in any central Pennsylvania counties until February 1st or 2nd. Widespread snowfall amounts of 10-15 inches were observed, with more than 20 inches of accumulation in portions of Schuylkill County. This was a Miller Type B storm, with the primarily low pushing up the Ohio Valley before a center-jump to the Mid-Atlantic coast was observed. Snow was the predominant precipitation type, although the Lower Susquehanna Valley had a several hour period of light wintry mix before the coastal low strengthened. The system then very slowly pulled east-northeastward along the southern New England coast and out to sea.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 8 to 12 inches of snow across Columbia County from January 31 to February 2, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm swept across the Northeastern US and brought a wintry mix to Central New York and Northeast Pennsylvania. A few counties received up to a half inch of ice. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulated up to 0.5 inches, mainly at the higher elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture-laden system developed over the Gulf States and pushes up the spine of the Appalachians, bring a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain to central Pennsylvania from the evening of February 15 into the morning of February 16. The Lower Susquehanna Valley quickly went over to plain rain after a brief period of freezing rain, while the central mountains had a prolonged period of sleet and freezing rain with many locations receiving over 0.25 of ice accretion. North of I-80, there was 1-2 inches of snow and sleet followed by freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced a quarter of an inch of ice accumulation, plus half an inch of sleet and an inch of snow to Warren County from February 15-16, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small MCS crossed the Laurel Highlands during the late morning hours of June 13, 2021, with wind damage being reported in Somerset County. Later in the day, additional showers and storms developed in the very warm and humid airmass over central Pennsylvania, ahead of an approaching cold front. These late-day storms produced sporadic wind damage and hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees onto wires in Avis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-day excessive heat event occurred across much of the mid-Atlantic from August 11-13, 2021. Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s combined with dew point values near 70 caused widespread heat index values near to above 105F on both August 11 and 12, and locally into the 13th before a cold front brought relief.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values reached 105F on August 11 and 12, and locally on the 13th. Some 110F values occurred on the 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southward moving cold front stalled across northern New Jersey and northeast Pennsylvania early on September 13. Warm and humid conditions were present south of the front. An upper level disturbance moving through the westerly flow aloft helped to increase wind shear. Combined with the instability, this disturbance caused a line of thunderstorms to move through the region during the evening hours of September 13. This line of storms became severe, with a number of reports of damaging wind along it. The line moved offshore and out of the region later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on wires in Beaver Meadows. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level shortwave trough crossed the Mid-Atlantic region on June 14, 2021, generating a line of storms that developed over northwestern Pennsylvania by late morning and progressed east-southeastward through the afternoon hours. Sporadic wind damage was observed with this line, with most of this occurring within a single, long-lived bowing segment that progressed from Elk County east-southeastward along the I-80 corridor to Columbia County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires near Saint Marys.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A near record warm air mass was in place over the mid-Atlantic on June 30. Combined with fairly high humidity, moderate to strong instability built over the region. An approaching surface trough acted to trigger scattered thunderstorms over the area during the afternoon and evening hours. Shear was not very strong, which limited overall storm organization. However, some storms did become strong to severe and produced instances of damaging winds. Convective activity diminished during the later evening hours with the loss of daytime heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed near Harrisburg School Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-day excessive heat event occurred across much of the mid-Atlantic near the end of June 2021. High temperatures in the mid to locally upper 90s combined with dew points in the upper 60s to near 70 caused heat index values to reach 105 to 110 over much of the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid air mass remained in place over the mid-Atlantic on June 9, following several days of unusually hot and humid conditions for the season. Similar to the previous day, afternoon thunderstorm development occurred in the mid-Atlantic mainly due to the influence of a weak cold front to the north and a weak surface trough. Like the previous day, storms were disorganized due to weak wind fields and a lack of forcing. However, the strong to extreme instability allowed some storms to briefly become severe with a threat for damaging winds, hail, and torrential rain. Convection gradually dissipated during the evening hours with the loss of daytime heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and wires were downed in the area of where Lower Mountain Rd, Pineville Rd, and Street Rd intersect. A social media photo also showed damage to a chimney. The damage was likely caused by a downburst. Time estimated from radar and dispatch reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two forest fires at opposite ends of Ohiopyle State Park in Fayette County consumed nearly 500 acres of woodlands on April 8th. A fire along South Dunbar Road burned more than 250 acres of state game land.  The second also burned more than 200 acres at the opposite end of the park near Victoria Flats and Rock Spring roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ed Callahan, district forester of Forbes State Forest, said it is among the worst forest fires in region in the past decade. The forest includes more than 60,000 acres stretching from Ohiopyle north through Somerset and Westmoreland counties.||The first fire, along Dunbar Road in a state game lands, was reported just after 5 p.m. Thursday and consumed more than 250 acres before firefighters were able to extinguish the flames at around 1 a.m. Friday, Fayette County 911 officials said.||A second fire was reported a short time later near Victoria Flats and Rock Spring roads. That blaze destroyed more than 200 acres of woods along the Youghiogheny River before crews were able to get it under control. Gusty winds that blew through the area Thursday helped spread the fires. 74 crews from departments in Westmoreland and Fayette responded to the incidents along with teams from the state���s forestry department.||Article: https://triblive.com/local/regional/ohiopyle-area-returning-to-normal-after-forest-fires/.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the mid-Atlantic late on September 8. Widespread showers and thunderstorms developed in a warm and humid air mass ahead of the front, moving into eastern Pennsylvania during the evening and early overnight hours. With instability waning after dark, much of the convection remained sub-severe. However, some instances of damaging wind did occur, particularly along a bowing line segment in Berks County. Showers and storms continued to gradually weaken as they shifted east later in the night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches and wires were downed in Exeter Twp. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm produced widespread snowfall across central Pennsylvania from January 31 to February 2, 2021. Although the snow began on January 31st, warning criteria was not met in any central Pennsylvania counties until February 1st or 2nd.  Widespread snowfall amounts of 10-15 inches were observed, with more than 20 inches of accumulation in portions of Schuylkill County. This was a Miller Type B storm, with the primarily low pushing up the Ohio Valley before a center-jump to the Mid-Atlantic coast was observed. Snow was the predominant precipitation type, although the Lower Susquehanna Valley had a several hour period of light wintry mix before the coastal low strengthened. The system then very slowly pulled east-northeastward along the southern New England coast and out to sea.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 11 to 14 inches of snow across Montour County from January 31 to February 2, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As strengthening low pressure tracked through the Great Lakes on March 28, a strong cold front approached the mid-Atlantic coast. A narrow ribbon of modest instability developed ahead of the front. With strong wind fields in place and strong forcing from the cold front, a line of convection developed. The minimal instability caused this to be a low-topped convective line with little, though not zero, lightning. However, the strong forcing and wind fields caused the line to become a fairly prolific producer of straight line wind damage. A number of severe wind gusts were measured, with numerous reports of tree, utility, and some structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto wires on Reservoir Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm produced widespread snowfall across central Pennsylvania from January 31 to February 2, 2021. Although the snow began on January 31st, warning criteria was not met in any central Pennsylvania counties until February 1st or 2nd.  Widespread snowfall amounts of 10-15 inches were observed, with more than 20 inches of accumulation in portions of Schuylkill County. This was a Miller Type B storm, with the primarily low pushing up the Ohio Valley before a center-jump to the Mid-Atlantic coast was observed. Snow was the predominant precipitation type, although the Lower Susquehanna Valley had a several hour period of light wintry mix before the coastal low strengthened. The system then very slowly pulled east-northeastward along the southern New England coast and out to sea.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 8 to 10 inches of snow across Blair County from January 31 to February 2, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp cold front crossed the area during the afternoon of March 28, 2021, generating a solid line of gusty showers over western Pennsylvania that very gradually intensified as it progressed eastward across the commonwealth and eventually produced isolated thunder. This line eventually produced some wind damage, knocking down a barn in far eastern Lancaster County near Gap.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 75 mph caused a partial collapse of a barn and roof damage to another barn at 278 Millwood Road in Salisbury Township. One minor injury was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of an arctic front spawned bands of snow showers and squalls across northern Pennsylvania during the afternoon hours of January 22, 2021. Some of these squalls produced brief heavy snowfall, gusty winds, and reduced visibility to less than a quarter of a mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall briefly dropped visibility to under one quarter mile in northwestern Lycoming County. Wind gusts were estimated near 30 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of an arctic front spawned bands of snow showers and squalls across northern Pennsylvania during the afternoon hours of January 22, 2021. Some of these squalls produced brief heavy snowfall, gusty winds, and reduced visibility to less than a quarter of a mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall briefly dropped visibility to under one quarter mile in southern Tioga County. Wind gusts were estimated near 30 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm produced widespread snowfall across central Pennsylvania from January 31 to February 2, 2021. Although the snow began on January 31st, warning criteria was not met in any central Pennsylvania counties until February 1st or 2nd.  Widespread snowfall amounts of 10-15 inches were observed, with more than 20 inches of accumulation in portions of Schuylkill County. This was a Miller Type B storm, with the primarily low pushing up the Ohio Valley before a center-jump to the Mid-Atlantic coast was observed. Snow was the predominant precipitation type, although the Lower Susquehanna Valley had a several hour period of light wintry mix before the coastal low strengthened. The system then very slowly pulled east-northeastward along the southern New England coast and out to sea.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 9 to 18 inches of snow across Northumberland County from January 31 to February 2, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent surface low moved northeastward along a stationary front from northeast Indiana to far-southern Quebec during the wee hours through early afternoon of March 26th. Simultaneously, the trailing cold front swept eastward from near the Indiana/Ohio line to near Lake Champlain and northern Chesapeake Bay. A tight surface pressure gradient accompanying the low and rapid surface pressure rises behind the cold front contributed to damaging wind gusts in northwest Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Meadville Tribune reported numerous downed trees and wires in Crawford County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice storm impacted much of central Pennsylvania with anywhere from a trace of ice in the Lower Susquehanna Valley up to a half an inch of ice accumulation in the southern Alleghenies and Laurel Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice storm produced over a quarter of an inch (0.25) of ice accumulation in Cambria County. Ice accumulation knocked down a power line onto Scalp Avenue near Hostetler Road in Geistown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice storm impacted much of central Pennsylvania with anywhere from a trace of ice in the Lower Susquehanna Valley up to a half an inch of ice accumulation in the southern Alleghenies and Laurel Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice storm produced a half an inch (0.50) of ice accumulation at Seven Springs Ski Resort and a quarter of an inch (0.25) in Somerset Borough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty west winds developed following a cold frontal passage during the early morning hours of March 26, 2021. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were observed across the Laurel Highlands and the southern Alleghenies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high wind gust of 62 mph was measured by the ASOS at Johnstown Airport in Cambria County at 1026am on March 26, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty west winds developed following a cold frontal passage during the early morning hours of March 26, 2021. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were observed across the Laurel Highlands and the southern Alleghenies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high wind gust of 66 mph was measured by a Mesonet approximately 3 miles north-northeast of Garrett in Somerset County at 10am on March 26, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty west winds following a cold frontal passage knocked down a single tree from a steep embankment across the 3800 block of Lisburn Road in Cumberland County. The tree brought down power lines and struck a truck that was traveling southbound on Lisburn Road. There were two occupants in the truck, one of whom was killed by the falling tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty west winds (40mph) following a cold frontal passage knocked down a single tree from a steep embankment across the 3800 block of Lisburn Road in Cumberland County. The tree brought down power lines and struck a truck that was traveling southbound on Lisburn Road. There were two occupants in the truck, one of whom was killed by the falling tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture increased across the region on January 25th ahead of a digging trough across the central Plains. Persistent moisture advection and increasing vertical motion supported by frontogenesis yielded precipitation during the afternoon and evening hours. Surface temperatures remained below freezing at the onset, supporting a wintry mix due to warmer air aloft.  A light glaze of freezing rain - up to 0.10 - was observed in most areas as well as modest snow amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thin film of icing was observed on sidewalks and grassy surfaces.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light icing observed in the area, including the 911 center parking lot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stacked low pressure system moved from the Ohio Valley to Western New York State during the afternoon and evening of the 3rd. Meanwhile, an intensifying surface low pressure system tracked along the Mid-Atlantic coast. A deformation zone developed over Northeast Pennsylvania, which led to a band of moderate to heavy snow. The heaviest snowfall from this band occurred during the evening of the 3rd into the early morning hours of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 3 to 14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weakening low pressure tracking out of the Mississippi Valley passed west of the mid-Atlantic on January 26. Secondary low pressure developed near the Delmarva region late on the 26th, tracking east-northeastward and passing south of New England. Upper level ridging prevented much intensification of this secondary low, which combined with the decaying primary low to bring a poorly organized weather system to the mid-Atlantic. A light wintry mix of precipitation fell, including a mix of snow and freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow and freezing rain fell. The Mount Pocono ASOS (KMPO) measured 0.09 inches of ice accretion, with nearby snowfall totals of an inch or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weakening low pressure tracking out of the Mississippi Valley passed west of the mid-Atlantic on January 26. Secondary low pressure developed near the Delmarva region late on the 26th, tracking east-northeastward and passing south of New England. Upper level ridging prevented much intensification of this secondary low, which combined with the decaying primary low to bring a poorly organized weather system to the mid-Atlantic. A light wintry mix of precipitation fell, including a mix of snow and freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell according to CoCoRaHS observations in Jim Thorpe, and observations from surrounding areas suggest scattered to widespread ice accretion of 0.01 to 0.10 inches likely occurred.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow and freezing rain fell. Snowfall of 0.5 inches was reported in Bangor, and observations from surrounding areas suggest patchy icing of a few hundredths of an inch likely occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking through the Great Lakes began to redevelop over the interior mid-Atlantic on January 1. Meanwhile, surface high pressure retreated northward from New England into southern Canada. This created a typical cold air damming regime over the mid-Atlantic as warm air advected in aloft while cold air remained trapped at the surface. This caused precipitation over interior portions of the mid-Atlantic to begin as sleet and freezing rain before eventually changing over to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sleet and freezing rain were observed. The Lehigh Valley International Airport ASOS (KABE) measured 0.08 inches of ice accretion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm affected the mid-Atlantic from January 31 to February 2, 2021. Low pressure tracking out of the Ohio Valley weakened as it moved slowly eastward on the 31st. Meanwhile, as that low weakened, secondary low pressure began to develop off the coast of the Carolinas and tracked northward, just off the mid-Atlantic coast. The low steadily intensified as it moved up the coast in a classic ���Miller B��� storm evolution. The result was a significant and unusually long duration winter storm. Overrunning precipitation associated with the initial low over the Ohio Valley impacted the region on the 31st. A cold antecedent air mass allowed most of this precipitation to fall as snow. This produced widespread snow accumulation, though the overrunning precipitation was mostly of the light to moderate variety. Some areas experienced a lull in precipitation on the overnight of the 31st as the secondary low began to develop and a dry slot overspread a large area. In addition, a surge of warmer air both at the surface and aloft caused precipitation to change to sleet or rain in some areas, especially locations farthest south and east such as the Delmarva peninsula and southern New Jersey. However, as the secondary low strengthened, heavy precipitation developed on February 1, with intense banding and areas of extreme snowfall rates of 2 to 4 inches per hour in northern New Jersey and portions of eastern Pennsylvania. Precipitation eventually turned back to snow all the way to the coastline as colder air was drawn in from the north. The secondary low pressure nearly stalled off the mid-Atlantic coast, causing snow to continue into February 2, although by then snowfall rates were much lighter and additional accumulation was limited. Areas where precipitation remained all snow and that experienced the heavy banding saw extreme snowfall totals, with isolated amounts of near 3 feet of snow and a fairly widespread swath of in excess of 2 feet. Snow flurries even continued into February 3 around the backside of the slow moving low���s large circulation, before it finally departed and brought an end to one of the region���s most notable snow events in years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the county on January 31 and continued into February 1 and 2, when the heaviest snow would fall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of January 31st, weakening low pressure moved northward into the lower Ohio Valley.  Frontogenetic forcing allowed for some higher snow rates during the morning and early afternoon hours, particularly in the higher elevations.  Snow rates became lighter in the afternoon as support for lift weakened and lifted northward, and as energy began to transfer to a coastal low. Accumulations through midnight were generally limited to four inches or less.||This event continued into early February, when strengthening northwest flow increased cold advection and allowed additional accumulating snow showers to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow measurement of 4.5 inches was taken at midnight at the NWS office. Other reports across the county from spotter and the public ranged from 2 to 4 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The high report of 3.8 inches of accumulation through the evening hours was received from North Cranberry.  An inch was reported at Butler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm affected the mid-Atlantic from January 31 to February 2, 2021. Low pressure tracking out of the Ohio Valley weakened as it moved slowly eastward on the 31st. Meanwhile, as that low weakened, secondary low pressure began to develop off the coast of the Carolinas and tracked northward, just off the mid-Atlantic coast. The low steadily intensified as it moved up the coast in a classic ���Miller B��� storm evolution. The result was a significant and unusually long duration winter storm. Overrunning precipitation associated with the initial low over the Ohio Valley impacted the region on the 31st. A cold antecedent air mass allowed most of this precipitation to fall as snow. This produced widespread snow accumulation, though the overrunning precipitation was mostly of the light to moderate variety. Some areas experienced a lull in precipitation on the overnight of the 31st as the secondary low began to develop and a dry slot overspread a large area. In addition, a surge of warmer air both at the surface and aloft caused precipitation to change to sleet or rain in some areas, especially locations farthest south and east such as the Delmarva peninsula and southern New Jersey. However, as the secondary low strengthened, heavy precipitation developed on February 1, with intense banding and areas of extreme snowfall rates of 2 to 4 inches per hour in northern New Jersey and portions of eastern Pennsylvania. Precipitation eventually turned back to snow all the way to the coastline as colder air was drawn in from the north. The secondary low pressure nearly stalled off the mid-Atlantic coast, causing snow to continue into February 2, although by then snowfall rates were much lighter and additional accumulation was limited. Areas where precipitation remained all snow and that experienced the heavy banding saw extreme snowfall totals, with isolated amounts of near 3 feet of snow and a fairly widespread swath of in excess of 2 feet. Snow flurries even continued into February 3 around the backside of the slow moving low���s large circulation, before it finally departed and brought an end to one of the region���s most notable snow events in years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the county on January 31 and continued into February 1 and 2, when the heaviest snow would fall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of January 31st, weakening low pressure moved northward into the lower Ohio Valley.  Frontogenetic forcing allowed for some higher snow rates during the morning and early afternoon hours, particularly in the higher elevations.  Snow rates became lighter in the afternoon as support for lift weakened and lifted northward, and as energy began to transfer to a coastal low. Accumulations through midnight were generally limited to four inches or less.||This event continued into early February, when strengthening northwest flow increased cold advection and allowed additional accumulating snow showers to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of 1 to 3 inches of snow through the evening hours were received, highest near Aliquippa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking through the Great Lakes began to redevelop over the interior mid-Atlantic on January 1. Meanwhile, surface high pressure retreated northward from New England into southern Canada. This created a typical cold air damming regime over the mid-Atlantic as warm air advected in aloft while cold air remained trapped at the surface. This caused precipitation over interior portions of the mid-Atlantic to begin as sleet and freezing rain before eventually changing over to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain fell in the county. The Pocono Mountains Airport ASOS (KMPO) measured 0.27 inches of ice accretion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong moisture advection combined with crossing low pressure to produce precipitation on January 1st. Subfreezing temperatures during the morning were slow to warm during the day, creating a period of freezing rain across the region.  This freezing rain lasted the longest along east-facing slopes east of Pittsburgh, where upslope flow ahead of the advancing low allowed subfreezing temperatures to linger longer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of a light glaze of icing were received from across the county, including a trace at the PIT ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of upper level disturbances passing over the mid-Atlantic brought a somewhat long duration but fairly low impact winter weather event to the area from the morning of January 3 into the predawn hours of January 4. Multiple batches of warm-advection driven precipitation affected the region in association with these disturbances as weak surface cyclogenesis began to occur just offshore. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough over the eastern Great Lakes region allowed for light lake effect snow showers to develop during the late afternoon on January 17th. As colder air aloft and better surface convergence developed over eastern Lake Erie, lake effect snow became more organized into a single band during the early morning hours of the 18th. This band pushed into Northwest Pennsylvania before dawn and snowfall rates increased to one inch per hour across Erie County. As the band became more organized, snowfall rates increased briefly to 2 inches per hour and visibility decreased to a quarter mile or less through the late morning and early afternoon hours. Snowfall became so intense that an accident involving a jack-knifed tractor trailer occurred on Interstate 90 in North East Township and closed the highway for several hours. No deaths or injuries were reported with this accident or at all during the snow event. By late afternoon on the 18th, snow began diminishing across the area and quickly ended by evening. The highest snow total for this event was 19 inches in Greenfield Township. Other selected storm total snowfall amounts include: 18 inches in North East Township, 17.5 inches in Greene Township, 17 inches in the city of Erie, 14 inches at Erie International Airport, 11.3 inches in Millcreek Township, 9.6 inches in Summit Township, 8.7 inches in Lake City, 8.5 inches in North East, 7.5 inches in Corry, 7.1 inches in Harborcreek, 6.5 inches in McKean, 5 inches in Edinboro, and 4 inches in Union City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A surface trough over the eastern Great Lakes region allowed for light lake effect snow showers to develop during the late afternoon on January 17th. As colder air aloft and better surface convergence developed over eastern Lake Erie, lake effect snow became more organized into a single band during the early morning hours of the 18th. This band pushed into Northwest Pennsylvania before dawn and snowfall rates increased to one inch per hour across Erie County. As the band became more organized, snowfall rates increased briefly to 2 inches per hour and visibility decreased to a quarter mile or less through the late morning and early afternoon hours. By late afternoon on the 18th, snow began diminishing across the area and quickly ended by evening. The highest snow total for this event for the northern portion of Erie County was 17 inches in the city of Erie. Other selected storm total snowfall amounts include: 14 inches at Erie International Airport, 8.7 inches in Lake City, 8.5 inches in North East, and 7.1 inches in Harborcreek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light lake effect snow developed over Lake Erie and pushed over Northwest Pennsylvania during the early evening hours of January 19. A shortwave trough entered the region and enhanced this lake effect snow late on the 19th into the 20th and a brief period of one inch per hour snowfall rates were reported in Erie County. Dry air quickly entered during the morning of January 20 and snow dissipated across the region. During this brief snow event, 8 inches of snow fell in a 12 hour period in North East Township in Erie County. Other snow totals include: 7.4 inches in Greenfield Township, 6.5 inches in Greene Township, 6.3 inches in Summit Township, and 5 inches in McKean. Lesser amounts were recorded elsewhere in Erie County. Impacts with this event were minimal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure passed offshore of the mid-Atlantic on February 18. Another weak, secondary wave of low pressure tracked along a similar path along a frontal zone trailing behind the primary low on the 19th. Strong high pressure over New England provided a cold air mass over the mid-Atlantic, leading to wintry precipitation as these areas of low pressure tracked offshore. The heaviest precipitation occurred with the primary wave on the 18th, with many areas seeing snow, some locally heavy, with a change to sleet and rain towards coastal areas. A narrow but intense band of precipitation provided a maximum in snowfall over portions of southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. By the overnight of the 18th and into the 19th, precipitation became lighter, with a mix of light snow and light freezing rain or drizzle providing some additional accumulations and impacts. The system finally pulled away entirely by the late afternoon and evening of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell across the county, with an initial round of snow on the 18th, followed by a lull and some light freezing drizzle on the overnight of the 18th, then additional light snow on the 19th. Over the course of the event, a maximum storm total snowfall report of 5.8 inches was received from near Bossardsville. Several other 4 to 5 inch reports were received. The Mount Pocono (KMPO) ASOS recorded 0.04 inches of ice accretion from the freezing drizzle on the night of the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture-laden system developed over the Gulf States and pushes up the spine of the Appalachians, bring a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain to central Pennsylvania from the evening of February 15 into the morning of February 16. The Lower Susquehanna Valley quickly went over to plain rain after a brief period of freezing rain, while the central mountains had a prolonged period of sleet and freezing rain with many locations receiving over 0.25 of ice accretion. North of I-80, there was 1-2 inches of snow and sleet followed by freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice storm produced between 0.25 and 0.50 inches of ice accumulation across Somerset County from February 15-16, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm affected the mid-Atlantic from January 31 to February 2, 2021. Low pressure tracking out of the Ohio Valley weakened as it moved slowly eastward on the 31st. Meanwhile, as that low weakened, secondary low pressure began to develop off the coast of the Carolinas and tracked northward, just off the mid-Atlantic coast. The low steadily intensified as it moved up the coast in a classic ���Miller B��� storm evolution. The result was a significant and unusually long duration winter storm. Overrunning precipitation associated with the initial low over the Ohio Valley impacted the region on the 31st. A cold antecedent air mass allowed most of this precipitation to fall as snow. This produced widespread snow accumulation, though the overrunning precipitation was mostly of the light to moderate variety. Some areas experienced a lull in precipitation on the overnight of the 31st as the secondary low began to develop and a dry slot overspread a large area. In addition, a surge of warmer air both at the surface and aloft caused precipitation to change to sleet or rain in some areas, especially locations farthest south and east such as the Delmarva peninsula and southern New Jersey. However, as the secondary low strengthened, heavy precipitation developed on February 1, with intense banding and areas of extreme snowfall rates of 2 to 4 inches per hour in northern New Jersey and portions of eastern Pennsylvania. Precipitation eventually turned back to snow all the way to the coastline as colder air was drawn in from the north. The secondary low pressure nearly stalled off the mid-Atlantic coast, causing snow to continue into February 2, although by then snowfall rates were much lighter and additional accumulation was limited. Areas where precipitation remained all snow and that experienced the heavy banding saw extreme snowfall totals, with isolated amounts of near 3 feet of snow and a fairly widespread swath of in excess of 2 feet. Snow flurries even continued into February 3 around the backside of the slow moving low���s large circulation, before it finally departed and brought an end to one of the region���s most notable snow events in years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the county on January 31. It became heavy on February 1 as intense banding developed over the Lehigh Valley. Lighter snow continued into February 2 before the storm finally departed. By the end of the event, a report was received from Coopersburg of 31.0 inches of snow. Numerous other reports of 24 to 30 inches of snow were received in the major winter storm. The Lehigh Valley International Airport recorded a storm total of 27.3 inches of snow.||A 67 year old woman with Alzheimer's died of hypothermia in Allentown when she wandered outside during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the mid-Atlantic on February 22, with a developing wave of low pressure tracking northeast along the front just inland from the Atlantic coast. This fast moving system brought a period of steady precipitation to the region mainly during the morning and midday hours on the 22nd. A cold air mass was in place ahead of the system, causing precipitation to start as snow. Southerly winds ahead of the cold front brought warmer air into the region, causing a change from snow to rain in many areas. Despite it being a fairly atypical setup for wintry precipitation in the region, a brief but intense period of snow affected much of eastern Pennsylvania and northern New Jersey. Several inches of heavy, wet snow fell in just a few hours, causing considerable disruption on area roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of snow fell, which changed to freezing rain near the end of the event. A report of 4.6 inches of snow was received from Bartonsville. The Mount Pocono Airport (KMPO) ASOS reported 0.03 inches of ice accretion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm affected the mid-Atlantic from January 31 to February 2, 2021. Low pressure tracking out of the Ohio Valley weakened as it moved slowly eastward on the 31st. In addition, a surge of warmer air both at the surface and aloft caused precipitation to change to sleet or rain in some areas, especially locations farthest south and east such as the Delmarva peninsula and southern New Jersey. A widespread 20 to 30 inches of snow fell throughout the county in one of the area's largest winter storms on record.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed over the Great Lakes and up the Saint Lawrence River valley from February 22 into February 23, 2021, pushing a warm front and cold front across central Pennsylvania. A narrow, north-south stripe of heavy snow (1-2 per hour) associated with the warm front passed over the Susquehanna Valley during the morning rush hour, and the heavy snowfall rates combined with the potential for a flash freeze prompted the issuance of snow squall warnings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall on the leading edge of a mesoscale band of heavy snow briefly dropped visibility to under one quarter mile in and produced flash-freeze conditions in eastern Cumberland County.

Southerly winds ahead of the cold front brought warmer air into the region, causing a change from snow to rain in many areas. Despite it being a fairly atypical setup for wintry precipitation in the region, a brief but intense period of snow affected much of eastern Pennsylvania and northern New Jersey. Several inches of heavy, wet snow fell in just a few hours, causing considerable disruption on area roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell across the county, which steadily changed to rain as warmer air moved in. In the northern portion of the county, a report of 3.3 inches of snow was received from Chestnut Hill. Further south, Philadelphia International Airport (KPHL) reported just 0.8 inches of snow. Both KPHL and Northeast Philadelphia Airport (KPNE) reported traces of freezing rain as snow changed to rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the mid-Atlantic on February 22, with a developing wave of low pressure tracking northeast along the front just inland from the Atlantic coast. This fast moving system brought a period of steady precipitation to the region mainly during the morning and midday hours on the 22nd. A cold air mass was in place ahead of the system, causing precipitation to start as snow. Southerly winds ahead of the cold front brought warmer air into the region, causing a change from snow to rain in many areas. Despite it being a fairly atypical setup for wintry precipitation in the region, a brief but intense period of snow affected much of eastern Pennsylvania and northern New Jersey. Several inches of heavy, wet snow fell in just a few hours, causing considerable disruption on area roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of snow fell, which was heavy at times before changing to rain. A trained spotter in Newtown Square reported 4.0 inches of snow before the changeover, with another spotter report of 4.0 inches in Wayne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm affected the mid-Atlantic from January 31 to February 2, 2021. Low pressure tracking out of the Ohio Valley weakened as it moved slowly eastward on the 31st. Meanwhile, as that low weakened, secondary low pressure began to develop off the coast of the Carolinas and tracked northward, just off the mid-Atlantic coast. The low steadily intensified as it moved up the coast in a classic ���Miller B��� storm evolution. The result was a significant and unusually long duration winter storm. Overrunning precipitation associated with the initial low over the Ohio Valley impacted the region on the 31st. A cold antecedent air mass allowed most of this precipitation to fall as snow. This produced widespread snow accumulation, though the overrunning precipitation was mostly of the light to moderate variety. Some areas experienced a lull in precipitation on the overnight of the 31st as the secondary low began to develop and a dry slot overspread a large area. In addition, a surge of warmer air both at the surface and aloft caused precipitation to change to sleet or rain in some areas, especially locations farthest south and east such as the Delmarva peninsula and southern New Jersey. However, as the secondary low strengthened, heavy precipitation developed on February 1, with intense banding and areas of extreme snowfall rates of 2 to 4 inches per hour in northern New Jersey and portions of eastern Pennsylvania. Precipitation eventually turned back to snow all the way to the coastline as colder air was drawn in from the north. The secondary low pressure nearly stalled off the mid-Atlantic coast, causing snow to continue into February 2, although by then snowfall rates were much lighter and additional accumulation was limited. Areas where precipitation remained all snow and that experienced the heavy banding saw extreme snowfall totals, with isolated amounts of near 3 feet of snow and a fairly widespread swath of in excess of 2 feet. Snow flurries even continued into February 3 around the backside of the slow moving low���s large circulation, before it finally departed and brought an end to one of the region���s most notable snow events in years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began on January 31. Precipitation continued into February 1. It decreased in intensity and also mixed with sleet for a time before changing back to snow later on February 1, when it also became heavy at times. Lighter snow continued into February 2. A trained spotter in Villanova reported a storm total of 9.0 inches of snow. Several other reports of 6 to 9 inch storm totals were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Ida impacted the mid-Atlantic on September 1, 2021. As it moved northward, Ida began transitioning to a strong mid-latitude cyclone with pronounced frontal features, but with continued tropical moisture. On a regional level, the remnants of Ida became one of the most severe natural disasters in US history, due to a combination of several tornadoes, some of them strong, as well as catastrophic flooding over a large and densely populated area. The severe weather threat unfolded as the system's developing warm front lifted northward from the Delmarva region into Pennsylvania and New Jersey. South of the warm front, partial clearing and moderate to strong instability developed. Meanwhile, very strong shear and forcing, along with tropical moisture, were present within the warm sector. The result was widespread convection in the form of both supercell and quasi-linear thunderstorms. A number of storms became severe across the region, producing several tornadoes and other instances of wind damage. This continued an exceptionally active stretch of high end severe weather in the region, coming barely a month after the tornado outbreak of July 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado embedded within a squall line developed several miles |southeast of the storm which produced a tornado in Buckingham |Township. Tornadic damage was first observed in the vicinity of |Stoneybrook Rd at Eagle Rd, near Jericho Creek. The tornado |tracked northeastward from there. It passed over the Jericho |National Golf Club, and sporadic tree damage mainly consisting of |uproots and large limbs snapped was observed in a path throughout |the golf course. It then moved over the adjacent Brownsburg Park, |where additional tree damage was observed and several soccer goals|were observed flipped in various directions. The tornado then |began to approach the Delaware River. Some minor tree damage was |observed along River Rd. However, the tornado appeared to lift |just slightly before reaching the Delaware River, with no apparent|damage on Pebbletown Rd just prior to the river. Throughout its |path, the tree damage produced by this tornado was fairly |sporadic, and it may have been discontinuous. Noticeable wind |damage was also observed for at least a mile east of the tornado |track, especially near its endpoint close to the Delaware River. |This is believed to be straight line wind damage, which is |corroborated by radar data, occurring around the rear flank |downdraft region of the tornadic circulation. However, the exact |length and width of the tornadic circulation is somewhat uncertain|due to the surrounding areas of straight line wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak wave of low pressure tracked over the mid-Atlantic on February 9. The low was fast moving and had poor dynamic forcing associated with it, limiting precipitation production. However, the air mass was sufficiently cold for what precipitation did occur to fall as mainly snow over northern New Jersey and portions of eastern Pennsylvania, with some patchy light freezing rain and drizzle reported as well. Some light snow accumulations resulted mainly during the morning and midday hours of February 9.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell across the county, accumulation 3 to 5 inches. Reports of 5.0 inches of snow were received from both Kunkletown and Pocono Pines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure tracked offshore of the mid-Atlantic on February 13, spreading some light precipitation into the region. A surge of warm air aloft prevented the light precipitation from falling as snow. However, strong high pressure to the north promoted a cold air damming environment with sub-freezing air trapped at the surface. This led to most of the precipitation falling as freezing rain, with some generally light ice accretion across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain fell. The Doylestown Airport (KDYL) ASOS reported 0.01 inches of ice accretion.
