EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked just east of the Great Lakes from the overnight hours of February 15 into the morning of the 16th. The low produced widespread precipitation over the mid-Atlantic. The inland track of the low allowed warmer air to surge northward for locations east of the center. However, lingering sub-freezing surface temperatures were present in some areas following recent cold weather. This caused precipitation to fall as freezing rain in portions of the interior mid-Atlantic. A lack of high pressure to the north to lock in colder air caused a quick change to plain rain in many areas. However, portions of the interior, especially in the vicinity of higher terrain, experienced more significant icing as freezing rain lasted for longer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain fell. No direct measurements were received, but observations from surrounding areas suggest a trace to a few hundredths of an inch of glaze ice likely accumulated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture-laden system developed over the Gulf States and pushes up the spine of the Appalachians, bring a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain to central Pennsylvania from the evening of February 15 into the morning of February 16. The Lower Susquehanna Valley quickly went over to plain rain after a brief period of freezing rain, while the central mountains had a prolonged period of sleet and freezing rain with many locations receiving over 0.25 of ice accretion. North of I-80, there was 1-2 inches of snow and sleet followed by freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced a quarter of an inch of ice accumulation, plus half an inch of sleet and an inch of snow across Sullivan County from February 15-16, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed over the Great Lakes and up the Saint Lawrence River valley from February 22 into February 23, 2021, pushing a warm front and cold front across central Pennsylvania. A narrow, north-south stripe of heavy snow (1-2 per hour) associated with the warm front passed over the Susquehanna Valley during the morning rush hour, and the heavy snowfall rates combined with the potential for a flash freeze prompted the issuance of snow squall warnings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall on the leading edge of a mesoscale band of heavy snow briefly dropped visibility to under one quarter mile in and produced flash-freeze conditions in southern Lycoming County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance accompanied by a weak wave of surface low pressure passed south of the Delmarva peninsula in the early hours of February 11. Precipitation blossomed ahead of and north of this disturbance. Over the mid-Atlantic, temperatures were cold enough for this to fall as a fluffy light snow in most areas. The relatively weak nature of the system combined with considerable dry air in place limited the amount of precipitation that fell. However, good snow to liquid ratios and the predawn timing of most of the snow allowed for efficient accumulation, with a widespread area of 3 to 5 inches of snow over the mid-Atlantic and some amounts locally a little higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell. A report of 3.0 inches of snow was received from Lower Makefield Twp, and a trained spotter in Furlong reported 2.9 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the Gulf Coast states and progressed northeastward and off the Carolina coast on February 18, 2021, pushing a swath of generally light snow across central Pennsylvania. The highest snowfall totals occurred near the Mason-Dixon line in southern York and southern Lancaster counties, where 6 to 9 inches of snow accumulated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 6 to 9 inches of snow across southern York County, most of which fell over an 11-hour period from 1am to 12pm on Thursday, February 18, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving wave of low pressure tracked northeastward off the Atlantic coast on February 7. The low steadily intensified as it gained latitude, and a sizeable shield of banded precipitation developed in its northwest quadrant. This resulted in a fairly short duration period of precipitation over the eastern mid-Atlantic, but with precipitation rates that were moderate to heavy at times. The storm produced a wide swath of several inches of snow, with considerable local variations in the snow totals. The variations were due to both distinct banding features as well as marginally cold temperatures especially near the coast, which caused precipitation to start as rain in some areas. The storm departed the region by the late afternoon of February 7 after producing a general 4 to 8 inches of snow, with some localized totals that were a little higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell across the county, with a maximum report of 4.6 inches from a trained spotter in Forks Twp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving wave of low pressure tracked northeastward off the Atlantic coast on February 7. The low steadily intensified as it gained latitude, and a sizeable shield of banded precipitation developed in its northwest quadrant. This resulted in a fairly short duration period of precipitation over the eastern mid-Atlantic, but with precipitation rates that were moderate to heavy at times. The storm produced a wide swath of several inches of snow, with considerable local variations in the snow totals. The variations were due to both distinct banding features as well as marginally cold temperatures especially near the coast, which caused precipitation to start as rain in some areas. The storm departed the region by the late afternoon of February 7 after producing a general 4 to 8 inches of snow, with some localized totals that were a little higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell across the area and was heavy at times as strong banding and high snow rates developed over southeast Pennsylvania. Bucks County saw the highest snow totals in the state, with a report of 10.0 inches of snow from New Britain and several reports in excess of 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked just east of the Great Lakes from the overnight hours of February 15 into the morning of the 16th. The low produced widespread precipitation over the mid-Atlantic. The inland track of the low allowed warmer air to surge northward for locations east of the center. However, lingering sub-freezing surface temperatures were present in some areas following recent cold weather. This caused precipitation to fall as freezing rain in portions of the interior mid-Atlantic. A lack of high pressure to the north to lock in colder air caused a quick change to plain rain in many areas. However, portions of the interior, especially in the vicinity of higher terrain, experienced more significant icing as freezing rain lasted for longer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain fell. A trained spotter in North Catasauqua reported 0.17 inches of ice accretion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Instability was able to build ahead of a cold front on May 26th.  CAPE values of 1500 to 2000 J/kg were achieved by afternoon, with sufficient wind shear and frontal support for lines of strong to severe thunderstorms.  Although a few reports of penny to quarter-sized hail were received, wind damage was the primary impact. Many reports of downed trees were received, causing scattered power outages and blocked roads.  A few reports of damage to cars and structures were received, with a patio roof blown off in Greensburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported to be blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong weather system affected the mid-Atlantic on April 30, 2021. A cold front moved through the region in the predawn hours of April 30, associated with low pressure moving into coastal New England. An upper level disturbance and a secondary cold front then approached late in the day on the 30th. This system interacted with the departing low pressure over New England, causing that low to rapidly intensify as it began moving towards the Canadian maritimes. With high pressure over the Midwest, this led to a very strong pressure gradient over the mid-Atlantic. Combined with strong dynamics associated with the secondary cold front, a lengthy period of windy conditions developed for much of the day on the 30th. Widespread wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph, with some gusts of 60 to 65 mph, occurred. This, combined with many trees across the region already bearing leaves, led to numerous instances of tree damage and power outages across the region. Scattered showers associated with the secondary front also helped to locally enhance wind damage. Winds finally began to decrease by the night of the 30th as the gradient gradually weakened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some downed trees and power lines were reported, including on Kunkletown Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down north of Weedville, Elk County on the afternoon of May 4, 2021. The tornado produced maximum winds estimated near 90 mph along a path that was just over half a mile in length and had a maximum path width of approximately 125 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service confirmed that an EF1 tornado occurred in Elk County at approximately 2:51 pm on May 4, 2021. Photo evidence shows tree damage concentrated along a narrow area along Old Byrnedale Rd and Route 255 about 2.5 miles NNW of Weedville, PA. Several trees were uprooted or snapped. This is likely near the end of the damage path, based on weakening low level rotation and updraft strength observed by radar. Video evidence from Weedville shows a rapidly rotating funnel approximately 0.5 miles WSW of Route 255. A ridge line obstructed the view of the ground beneath the funnel. However, a radar-indicated tornado debris signature confirms lofted debris and subsequently the occurrence of a tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed near and north of the Baltimore, MD area in an environment of moderate instability and wind shear ahead of a cold front late on April 29. Cloud cover and showers from earlier in the day limited instability for most of the day, but some late day heating allowed sufficient destabilization for a few cells to develop. Storms organized into a line segment and tracked east-northeast into southeast Pennsylvania and south-central New Jersey. The storms became severe for a time, including while passing in and around the Philadelphia metro area, before gradually weakening as they tracked into lesser instability further east. A few reports of damaging wind were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed near Summit Ln in Chester Heights. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed well ahead of an approaching cold front during the afternoon of May 26, 2021. Discrete storms developed over the central mountains, likely aided by a lee-side trough, and these cells eventually congealed into a line that crossed the Lower Susquehanna Valley. Well behind this line, another line of storms formed just ahead of the approaching front. Wind damage was the primary weather hazard throughout this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced quarter-sized hail in Benton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front approached the mid-Atlantic coast on April 21. Ahead of the front, modest instability developed in a strongly sheared and strongly forced environment. This led to the development of convection along and ahead of the front as it moved through during the mid afternoon hours. Mixed modes of linear and discrete storm cells were present, some of which became strong to severe. Scattered instances of hail and wind damage were reported across much of the eastern mid-Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured by Pennsylvania DOT RWIS station at I-95 and Girard Ave.

Well behind this line, another line of storms formed just ahead of the approaching front. Wind damage was the primary weather hazard throughout this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees onto Old Route 22 near Healy Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong weather system affected the mid-Atlantic on April 30, 2021. A cold front moved through the region in the predawn hours of April 30, associated with low pressure moving into coastal New England. An upper level disturbance and a secondary cold front then approached late in the day on the 30th. This system interacted with the departing low pressure over New England, causing that low to rapidly intensify as it began moving towards the Canadian maritimes. With high pressure over the Midwest, this led to a very strong pressure gradient over the mid-Atlantic. Combined with strong dynamics associated with the secondary cold front, a lengthy period of windy conditions developed for much of the day on the 30th. Widespread wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph, with some gusts of 60 to 65 mph, occurred. This, combined with many trees across the region already bearing leaves, led to numerous instances of tree damage and power outages across the region. Scattered showers associated with the secondary front also helped to locally enhance wind damage. Winds finally began to decrease by the night of the 30th as the gradient gradually weakened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of downed trees and wires in the county, including on PA-54 and Lentz Trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers supported by a crossing shortwave trough moved across western Pennsylvania on the evening of May 3rd.  Due to an overall lack of instability, lightning was not observed from this precipitation.  However, enough low-level shear existed to induce rotation in a few of the showers, and a very brief tornado occurred northwest of Portersville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on observed threshold damage to large limbs and in light of|radar data, a NWS storm survey concluded that the damage in|northeastern Lawrence County likely was the result of a very brief,|weak tornado that lasted only a short period of time.||Although radar velocity appeared at its strongest subsequent to|this time, rotation-track transects discovered no damage|downstream from this initial spot or into Butler County.||Photographs that have been shared with us appear to depict a wall|cloud with rising cloud debris earlier in the storm's track near|Bessemer, but no circulation can be confirmed at ground level. |NWS Pittsburgh is unaware of damage from western Lawrence County,|and therefore cannot confirm a tornado elsewhere along the |storm's path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary boundary was in place over southwest Pennsylvania on May 24th.  Taking advantage of an abundance of moisture and upper shortwave support, showers and thunderstorms developed and tracked along the boundary.  A couple of flooding events occurred in the urbanized areas of Allegheny County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A female was rescued after her car became stuck in high water near the Verizon Building at 590 Union Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Instability was able to build ahead of a cold front on May 26th.  CAPE values of 1500 to 2000 J/kg were achieved by afternoon, with sufficient wind shear and frontal support for lines of strong to severe thunderstorms.  Although a few reports of penny to quarter-sized hail were received, wind damage was the primary impact. Many reports of downed trees were received, causing scattered power outages and blocked roads.  A few reports of damage to cars and structures were received, with a patio roof blown off in Greensburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Friday, March 26th, a strong surface low pressure system tracked along Lake Ontario and brought strong winds across Northeastern Pennsylvania. Peak wind gusts of 35-45 mph were common with some gusts exceeding 55 mph.

Strengthening low pressure tracked across the Great Lakes and into the northeast CONUS on March 26th.  With the surface low track and low-level jet positioning both favorable, strong wind gusts were able to develop in the deep mixing behind a cold front. Numerous surface wind gusts in the 40 to 60 MPH range were recorded from the predawn hours into the early afternoon.||Widespread reports of tree and wire damage were received from southwest Pennsylvania. A few incidents of structural damage were noted, chiefly in Beaver and Allegheny Counties. West Penn Power reported over 17,000 customers without power at one point during the morning, with Duquesne Light seeing 6,767 outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and power lines were reported to be down throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a significant heatwave, a cold front approached the mid-Atlantic on July 1, 2021. A potent mid-level trough was also digging southward through southern Ontario and into the Great Lakes region, and a strong upper level jet streak was present downstream the trough axis, over the interior mid-Atlantic and northern New England. This strong upper level forcing combined with the approaching front and a lingering warm, moist air mass ahead of it caused widespread thunderstorm development on July 1. Storms occurred almost exclusively south of Interstate 78, in the environment of best instability. A number of storms became severe, taking the form of mainly linear and multi-cellular clusters but with some embedded supercellular characteristics. Straight line wind damage was the primary hazard, with a number of reports of downed trees and power outages due to winds. With the cold front remaining to the west, some storm activity continued into the overnight, but storm coverage and intensity gradually waned with the loss of daytime heating and the overturning of the atmosphere from earlier storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An mPing report of tree limbs or power lines down near Route 100. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong storms developed along a trailing cold front on the afternoon of June 21st. The environment had MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg, effective shear near 35kts, and steep low-level lapse rates. A brief embedded tornado developed within the line and crossed into the Seven Fields, PA region. Model hodographs noted this threat prior to occurrence, indicating that storm motion towards the southeast would be ideal for ingesting streamwise vorticity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Employee reported large limbs down on Route 19 and near the Interchange of 79/76.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the mid-Atlantic on July 21. Ahead of the front, an environment of moderate wind shear and seasonably warm and humid conditions promoted widespread thunderstorm development. Storms mainly took the form of multi-cell clusters and quasi-linear segments. A number of storms became severe, posing a threat for both damaging winds and, thanks to cold air aloft, large hail as they moved towards the coast. With the front moving through during the mid to late afternoon hours, most of the storm activity was offshore by early evening, with cooler and drier weather behind the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed on Carter Ave in Chester Twp. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed over the Lower Susquehanna Valley during the afternoon of July 8, 2021, in a very warm and humid airmass between a stationary front over New York state and an approaching Tropical Strom (Elsa) over central North Carolina. A few of these storms produced sporadic wind damage. This activity diminished as it pushed northward into a more stable, rain-cooled environment during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Paradise Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An advancing cold front, along with shortwave support, created the chance for thunderstorms across the region. Sufficient shear (40kts in the 0-6km layer), moderate to high instability (1500-2000 J/kg of CAPE), and dry mid-level air resulted in damaging winds and hail. Flash flooding occurred, mostly south of Pittsburgh, with training storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to an inch and half hail reported via the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought locally heavy rain to parts of southeastern Pennsylvania on the afternoon of July 12.  Rainfall totals up to 6 to 10 inches occurred in Northeast Philadelphia and in far lower Bucks County prompting the issuance of a Flash Flood Emergency.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread significant flooding occurred in Bensalem Township.  There were many road closures including parts of Interstate 95, US Route 13, Woodhaven Road and State Road.  Many individuals were trapped in vehicles by the flood waters, with numerous water rescues becoming necessary.  Flood waters reached houses including some along Allen Avenue, McKinley Avenue and Kings Avenue, where residents needed to be rescued from their homes.  The Lafayette Garden Apartments along US Route 13 at the Poquessing Creek were severely flooded with 80 to 100 units affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Elsa approached the mid-Atlantic states from the south on July 8. Downstream of Elsa, a warm and moist environment was present. A slow moving frontal boundary also resided over northern New Jersey, northern Pennsylvania, and into New York. This frontal system provided forcing for convective development over the mid-Atlantic, in fairly close proximity but unrelated to Tropical Storm Elsa. With strong instability and weak to moderate wind shear, some of the storms became severe as they organized into linear and multi-cellular clusters. Deep layer southerly flow in advance of Elsa caused storms to move northward as opposed to a more typical eastward motion. Damaging wind was the main hazard associated with these storms, with several strong downbursts noted. Storm activity eventually weakened and moved north of the region by evening as Elsa moved in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house was struck by lightning on Indian Trail with smoke reported in the house. This occurred from a morning round of sub-severe convection which preceded severe thunderstorms later in the day. Time estimated from dispatch report.

A line of strong storms developed along a trailing cold front on the afternoon of June 21st. The environment had MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg, effective shear near 35kts, and steep low-level lapse rates. A brief embedded tornado developed within the line and crossed into the Seven Fields, PA region. Model hodographs noted this threat prior to occurrence, indicating that storm motion towards the southeast would be ideal for ingesting streamwise vorticity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media reported large tree blocking the Blvd of the Allies.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions existed over the mid-Atlantic on July 6, with a cold front well northwest over the Great Lakes and southern Canada. As temperatures rose into the 90s with dew points in the upper 60s and low 70s, strong instability built over the region. A pre-frontal trough and a fast moving upper level disturbance provided some lift, which helped convection develop by mid afternoon. Convection was initially concentrated over northern Pennsylvania and New York, near the better forcing, but propagated southeastward with time. Shear was weak to moderate but sufficient to allow some storm organization into multi-cellular and linear segments. A number of storms became severe, producing damaging winds and some hail. As storms moved southeast, outflow boundaries moving even further south helped ignite additional convection, some severe, which continued into the early overnight hours. The early overnight convection was also noted to be especially prolific with regard to lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were downed on Jefferson Ave. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought locally heavy rain to parts of southeastern Pennsylvania on the afternoon of July 12.  Rainfall totals up to 6 to 10 inches occurred in Northeast Philadelphia and in far lower Bucks County prompting the issuance of a Flash Flood Emergency.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A water rescue took place in Darby Township along Hook Road near Industrial Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and very humid air mass was in place over the mid-Atlantic on July 17. A warm front which had sagged south into the region on the night of the 16th pushed back to the north on the morning of the 17th. Meanwhile, a cold front slowly approached by evening, with a pre-frontal surface trough ahead of it. The presence of multiple synoptic and mesoscale boundaries led to a highly complex convective evolution. Several areas and multiple rounds of convection affected the eastern mid-Atlantic over about a 12 hour period from early afternoon on the 17th to a little after midnight on the 18th. With shear falling in the weak to moderate range, convection mainly took the form of multi-cellular and linear clusters, though some semi-discrete supercellular structures were noted at times. A number of storms became severe with damaging wind. Areas of heavy rain and flash flooding also occurred due to the widespread and at times slow moving nature of the convection. Eventually, by later in the night, the approach of the cold front and significant overturning of the atmosphere from the day's convection caused remaining activity to weaken and shift offshore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An advancing cold front, along with shortwave support, created the chance for thunderstorms across the region. Sufficient shear (40kts in the 0-6km layer), moderate to high instability (1500-2000 J/kg of CAPE), and dry mid-level air resulted in damaging winds and hail. Flash flooding occurred, mostly south of Pittsburgh, with training storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast Media reported multiple power lines knocked down southeast of New Castle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a significant heatwave, a cold front approached the mid-Atlantic on July 1, 2021. A potent mid-level trough was also digging southward through southern Ontario and into the Great Lakes region, and a strong upper level jet streak was present downstream the trough axis, over the interior mid-Atlantic and northern New England. This strong upper level forcing combined with the approaching front and a lingering warm, moist air mass ahead of it caused widespread thunderstorm development on July 1. Storms occurred almost exclusively south of Interstate 78, in the environment of best instability. A number of storms became severe, taking the form of mainly linear and multi-cellular clusters but with some embedded supercellular characteristics. Straight line wind damage was the primary hazard, with a number of reports of downed trees and power outages due to winds. With the cold front remaining to the west, some storm activity continued into the overnight, but storm coverage and intensity gradually waned with the loss of daytime heating and the overturning of the atmosphere from earlier storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on US-1. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado impacted Laurel Hill State Park during the afternoon of July 29, 2021. The tornado had estimated peak winds of just over 100 mph, a path length of just under a quarter of a mile, and a maximum path width of 65 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado impacted Laurel Hill State Park during the afternoon of July 29, 2021. The tornado had estimated peak winds of just over 100mph, a path length of just under a quarter of a mile, and a maximum path width of 65 yards. Numerous trees along it's path were uprooted in various directions, while a few were also snapped, resulting in damage to some of the conservation buildings. Estimated maximum winds reached 102 mph as the tornado tracked through this portion of the park, before turning to the east and producing additional tree damage east of Laurel Hill Park Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions existed over the mid-Atlantic on July 6, with a cold front well northwest over the Great Lakes and southern Canada. As temperatures rose into the 90s with dew points in the upper 60s and low 70s, strong instability built over the region. A pre-frontal trough and a fast moving upper level disturbance provided some lift, which helped convection develop by mid afternoon. Convection was initially concentrated over northern Pennsylvania and New York, near the better forcing, but propagated southeastward with time. Shear was weak to moderate but sufficient to allow some storm organization into multi-cellular and linear segments. A number of storms became severe, producing damaging winds and some hail. As storms moved southeast, outflow boundaries moving even further south helped ignite additional convection, some severe, which continued into the early overnight hours. The early overnight convection was also noted to be especially prolific with regard to lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed in the area. Report via social media. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and very humid air mass was in place over the mid-Atlantic on July 17. A warm front which had sagged south into the region on the night of the 16th pushed back to the north on the morning of the 17th. Meanwhile, a cold front slowly approached by evening, with a pre-frontal surface trough ahead of it. The presence of multiple synoptic and mesoscale boundaries led to a highly complex convective evolution. Several areas and multiple rounds of convection affected the eastern mid-Atlantic over about a 12 hour period from early afternoon on the 17th to a little after midnight on the 18th. With shear falling in the weak to moderate range, convection mainly took the form of multi-cellular and linear clusters, though some semi-discrete supercellular structures were noted at times. A number of storms became severe with damaging wind. Areas of heavy rain and flash flooding also occurred due to the widespread and at times slow moving nature of the convection. Eventually, by later in the night, the approach of the cold front and significant overturning of the atmosphere from the day's convection caused remaining activity to weaken and shift offshore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downed tree blocked the road on Round Hill Rd at Kaoli Rd. Time estimated from radar and dispatch report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Elsa approached the mid-Atlantic states from the south on July 8. Downstream of Elsa, a warm and moist environment was present. A slow moving frontal boundary also resided over northern New Jersey, northern Pennsylvania, and into New York. This frontal system provided forcing for convective development over the mid-Atlantic, in fairly close proximity but unrelated to Tropical Storm Elsa. With strong instability and weak to moderate wind shear, some of the storms became severe as they organized into linear and multi-cellular clusters. Deep layer southerly flow in advance of Elsa caused storms to move northward as opposed to a more typical eastward motion. Damaging wind was the main hazard associated with these storms, with several strong downbursts noted. Storm activity eventually weakened and moved north of the region by evening as Elsa moved in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed on Seaton Ross Rd at Ivywood Ln. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures in March and above normal to record high temperatures in early April in Northwest Pennsylvania allowed for area fruit crops to begin budding ahead of schedule in April. A low pressure system brought several rounds of snow to the region on April 20-21 and residual snow allowed for temperatures to cool considerably with low temperatures in the upper 20s to lower 30s into the morning of the 22nd. For area farmers, the combination of snow and cold damaged area grape crops in interior portions of Erie County, Pennsylvania. The area with the most damage was in portions of Harborcreek and North East Townships near Interstate 90. These vineyards were heavily damaged with an estimated 85 to 95% crop loss on the first shoots of grapes. In some areas, some of these first shoots were only slightly damaged but the second shoots of grapes were delayed after an unimpressive first batch of grapes. Local news reports relay that some of the area will have only half a grape crop for the year. Area apple and cherry crops were also damaged prompting smaller yields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure located in eastern Canada and slow moving low pressure approaching from the southeastern states resulted in a prolonged onshore flow along the Middle Atlantic coast.  Moderate flooding occurred along the tidal Delaware River and its tributaries on the evening of October 28.  There was moderate to major tidal flooding on the evening of October 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate flooding occurred along the tidal Delaware River and its tidal tributaries.  There were many road closures with the flood waters approaching numerous homes and businesses.  The tide gauge at Burlington, New Jersey reached a level of 10.49 feet MLLW.

Flash flooding occurred, mostly south of Pittsburgh, with training storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter size hail; hail fell steady for 5 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong storms developed along a trailing cold front on the afternoon of June 21st. The environment had MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg, effective shear near 35kts, and steep low-level lapse rates. A brief embedded tornado developed within the line and crossed into the Seven Fields, PA region. Model hodographs noted this threat prior to occurrence, indicating that storm motion towards the southeast would be ideal for ingesting streamwise vorticity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large branches snapped off at least 4 trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Steep lapse rates associated with upper level low pressure combined with moderate wind shear caused scattered convection to develop over the mid-Atlantic during the afternoon and evening of July 2. The overall meager dynamics prevented widespread severe weather. However, a few multi-cellular storm clusters did become strong to severe and produced a couple instances of damaging winds and small hail before waning with the loss of daytime heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on a house on Bryant St. Report via broadcast media and confirmed by county fire rescue. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a significant heatwave, a cold front approached the mid-Atlantic on July 1, 2021. A potent mid-level trough was also digging southward through southern Ontario and into the Great Lakes region, and a strong upper level jet streak was present downstream the trough axis, over the interior mid-Atlantic and northern New England. This strong upper level forcing combined with the approaching front and a lingering warm, moist air mass ahead of it caused widespread thunderstorm development on July 1. Storms occurred almost exclusively south of Interstate 78, in the environment of best instability. A number of storms became severe, taking the form of mainly linear and multi-cellular clusters but with some embedded supercellular characteristics. Straight line wind damage was the primary hazard, with a number of reports of downed trees and power outages due to winds. With the cold front remaining to the west, some storm activity continued into the overnight, but storm coverage and intensity gradually waned with the loss of daytime heating and the overturning of the atmosphere from earlier storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed near the intersection of Washington Ln and Pepper Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions existed over the mid-Atlantic on July 6, with a cold front well northwest over the Great Lakes and southern Canada. As temperatures rose into the 90s with dew points in the upper 60s and low 70s, strong instability built over the region. A pre-frontal trough and a fast moving upper level disturbance provided some lift, which helped convection develop by mid afternoon. Convection was initially concentrated over northern Pennsylvania and New York, near the better forcing, but propagated southeastward with time. Shear was weak to moderate but sufficient to allow some storm organization into multi-cellular and linear segments. A number of storms became severe, producing damaging winds and some hail. As storms moved southeast, outflow boundaries moving even further south helped ignite additional convection, some severe, which continued into the early overnight hours. The early overnight convection was also noted to be especially prolific with regard to lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and very humid air mass was in place over the mid-Atlantic on July 17. A warm front which had sagged south into the region on the night of the 16th pushed back to the north on the morning of the 17th. Meanwhile, a cold front slowly approached by evening, with a pre-frontal surface trough ahead of it. The presence of multiple synoptic and mesoscale boundaries led to a highly complex convective evolution. Several areas and multiple rounds of convection affected the eastern mid-Atlantic over about a 12 hour period from early afternoon on the 17th to a little after midnight on the 18th. With shear falling in the weak to moderate range, convection mainly took the form of multi-cellular and linear clusters, though some semi-discrete supercellular structures were noted at times. A number of storms became severe with damaging wind. Areas of heavy rain and flash flooding also occurred due to the widespread and at times slow moving nature of the convection. Eventually, by later in the night, the approach of the cold front and significant overturning of the atmosphere from the day's convection caused remaining activity to weaken and shift offshore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power outages were reported. Large branches were blown down and siding was also blown off a house. The spotter estimated winds above 60 mph. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought locally heavy rain to parts of southeastern Pennsylvania on the afternoon of July 12.  Rainfall totals up to 6 to 10 inches occurred in Northeast Philadelphia and in far lower Bucks County prompting the issuance of a Flash Flood Emergency.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread significant flooding occurred in Bristol Township.  There were many road closures including parts of US Route 13.  Many individuals were trapped in vehicles by the flood waters, with numerous water rescues becoming necessary including those along Walnut Avenue and along McKinley Street at Wilson Avenue.  Flood waters reached some buildings along US Route 13 in the Croyden section of the township.  Part of Dixon Avenue near Newportville Road was washed out, and residents needed to be evacuated from nearby homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Elsa approached the mid-Atlantic states from the south on July 8. Downstream of Elsa, a warm and moist environment was present. A slow moving frontal boundary also resided over northern New Jersey, northern Pennsylvania, and into New York. This frontal system provided forcing for convective development over the mid-Atlantic, in fairly close proximity but unrelated to Tropical Storm Elsa. With strong instability and weak to moderate wind shear, some of the storms became severe as they organized into linear and multi-cellular clusters. Deep layer southerly flow in advance of Elsa caused storms to move northward as opposed to a more typical eastward motion. Damaging wind was the main hazard associated with these storms, with several strong downbursts noted. Storm activity eventually weakened and moved north of the region by evening as Elsa moved in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported south of Allentown. Time estimated from radar.

Although damaging winds were the main impact, a few storms also created large hail.  Flash flooding was reported from slow moving thunderstorms in Lycoming and Tioga Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding brought multiple road closures along Muncy Creek in Muncy Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening of July 16, 2021, in a very warm, humid airmass just south of a stalled frontal boundary that was draped across northern Pennsylvania. A few of these storms produced wind damage, including a particularly tall cell that produced winds estimated by a NWS Survey Team to be 85 to 95 mph in a wet microburst near Haneyville in northern Clinton County. Flash flooding from heavy rains also occurred in Northumberland and Sullivan Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a microburst just north of Haneyville with winds estimated to be 85 to 95 mph. That path length as approximately one mile, running from Coyote Ridge Road across Chickadee Lane and Coudersport Pike (Route 44) before ending near the intersection of Zinck Fork Road and Brown Run Road. The path width was approximately 75 yards. Multiple large trees were knocked down along the path, and one fell onto an unoccupied cabin on Chickadee Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms develop during the early afternoon hours of July 17, 2021 across western Pennsylvania and in the Lower Susquehanna Valley in an anomalously humid airmass ahead of an approaching cold front. The storms over the Lower Susquehanna Valley developed on a weak boundary in a high CAPE environment, and produced sporadic wind damage before moving to the east. The storms over western Pennsylvania eventually congealed into a broken line that progressed eastward across central Pennsylvania and had imbedded regions of damaging winds and very heavy rain. One hail report was received from beneath a particularly tall cell that developed over Blue Mountain just west of Marysville in Perry County.  Flash flooding from these storms was reported across McKean, Dauphin and Tioga Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees onto wires east of Burnham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An advancing cold front, along with shortwave support, created the chance for thunderstorms across the region. Sufficient shear (40kts in the 0-6km layer), moderate to high instability (1500-2000 J/kg of CAPE), and dry mid-level air resulted in damaging winds and hail. Flash flooding occurred, mostly south of Pittsburgh, with training storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media pictures of uprooted trees and blown apart swing set in Mars, PA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and very humid air mass was in place over the mid-Atlantic on July 17. A warm front which had sagged south into the region on the night of the 16th pushed back to the north on the morning of the 17th. Meanwhile, a cold front slowly approached by evening, with a pre-frontal surface trough ahead of it. The presence of multiple synoptic and mesoscale boundaries led to a highly complex convective evolution. Several areas and multiple rounds of convection affected the eastern mid-Atlantic over about a 12 hour period from early afternoon on the 17th to a little after midnight on the 18th. With shear falling in the weak to moderate range, convection mainly took the form of multi-cellular and linear clusters, though some semi-discrete supercellular structures were noted at times. A number of storms became severe with damaging wind. Areas of heavy rain and flash flooding also occurred due to the widespread and at times slow moving nature of the convection. Eventually, by later in the night, the approach of the cold front and significant overturning of the atmosphere from the day's convection caused remaining activity to weaken and shift offshore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed in North Catasauqua and Catasauqua. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms developed along an approaching front on Monday, June 14th. Strong shear (60kts in the 0-6km layer), moderate instability (CAPE of 1000-1500 J/kg), and dry mid-level air helped produce strong downdrafts and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Employee reported large tree branches down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong storms developed along a trailing cold front on the afternoon of June 21st. The environment had MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg, effective shear near 35kts, and steep low-level lapse rates. A brief embedded tornado developed within the line and crossed into the Seven Fields, PA region. Model hodographs noted this threat prior to occurrence, indicating that storm motion towards the southeast would be ideal for ingesting streamwise vorticity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 20 large trees snapped and uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in a moderate-to-high CAPE, but relatively low shear, environment over eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of July 6, 2021. Sporadic wind damage was reported from a few storms, along with half dollar-sized hail in Hershey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down multiple trees south of Campbelltown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires and produced structural damage on Stewart Street in Bellwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed during the afternoon and evening of the 12th in an environment of moderate CAPE and moderate shear southeast of a stationary front. Thunderstorms initially formed as single cells, then formed into multicell clusters later in the event over the Lower Susquehanna Valley.    Further north slower moving storms brought flooding around Mansfield in Tioga County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in a moderate CAPE, moderate shear environment just south of a warm front that was draped from northwest to southeast across central Pennsylvania during the afternoon of July 11, 2021. Several of the storms produced wind damage, and a few storms exhibited rotation near the warm front.   One slower moving thunderstorm brought urban flash flooding to Harrisburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm with winds estimated near 60 mph brought down multiple trees in Avis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe thunderstorms formed during the afternoon and evening within an environment of moderate CAPE and moderate shear. A shortwave moved across the Ohio Valley giving support for multiple rounds of convection, primarily in the form of multicell clusters and linear segments. Although damaging winds were the main impact, a few storms also created large hail.  Flash flooding was reported from slow moving thunderstorms in Lycoming and Tioga Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm brought hail estimated in excess of one inch to the State College area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening of July 16, 2021, in a very warm, humid airmass just south of a stalled frontal boundary that was draped across northern Pennsylvania. A few of these storms produced wind damage, including a particularly tall cell that produced winds estimated by a NWS Survey Team to be 85 to 95 mph in a wet microburst near Haneyville in northern Clinton County. Flash flooding from heavy rains also occurred in Northumberland and Sullivan Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding with water covering roads in Forks and Elkland Townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms develop during the early afternoon hours of July 17, 2021 across western Pennsylvania and in the Lower Susquehanna Valley in an anomalously humid airmass ahead of an approaching cold front. The storms over the Lower Susquehanna Valley developed on a weak boundary in a high CAPE environment, and produced sporadic wind damage before moving to the east. The storms over western Pennsylvania eventually congealed into a broken line that progressed eastward across central Pennsylvania and had imbedded regions of damaging winds and very heavy rain. One hail report was received from beneath a particularly tall cell that developed over Blue Mountain just west of Marysville in Perry County.  Flash flooding from these storms was reported across McKean, Dauphin and Tioga Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced quarter-sized hail in Bird-in-Hand.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1935 EDT Tioga County is activating their EOC to an enhanced level due to flooding conditions throughout the county. Trailer park on Benedict Lane in Roseville is being evacuated, one foot of water and rising. Rt 660 in Delmar Township is flooded. Route 6 in Mansfield is flooding. Numerous roadways washed out and flooded. |At 2020 EDT Roseville area at 73 Benedict Road (in Mansfield) a Mobile Home Park is flooding, 10 residents are being evacuated to the Mansfield Fire Department. Route 549 and Route 6 in Roseville are underwater in multiple locations. Route 549 is passable, but has substantial debris on the roadway from the flooding. At 2246 EDT a total of 38 persons were evacuated from the Roseville area this evening. Route 549 remains closed. Route 6 has reopened. Route 287 has reopened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms develop during the early afternoon hours of July 17, 2021 across western Pennsylvania and in the Lower Susquehanna Valley in an anomalously humid airmass ahead of an approaching cold front. The storms over the Lower Susquehanna Valley developed on a weak boundary in a high CAPE environment, and produced sporadic wind damage before moving to the east. The storms over western Pennsylvania eventually congealed into a broken line that progressed eastward across central Pennsylvania and had imbedded regions of damaging winds and very heavy rain. One hail report was received from beneath a particularly tall cell that developed over Blue Mountain just west of Marysville in Perry County.  Flash flooding from these storms was reported across McKean, Dauphin and Tioga Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees near Grampian.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An advancing cold front, along with shortwave support, created the chance for thunderstorms across the region. Sufficient shear (40kts in the 0-6km layer), moderate to high instability (1500-2000 J/kg of CAPE), and dry mid-level air resulted in damaging winds and hail. Flash flooding occurred, mostly south of Pittsburgh, with training storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of flooding south and east of Pittsburgh, numerous vehicles were stuck. In Whitehall, intersection of Provost and Wayman flooded near Saw Mill Run. Widespread flooding was reported in West Mifflin. In Monroeville, near  I-376 East, a vehicle was stranded on ramp. A few streets were flooded in Swissvale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong storms developed along a trailing cold front on the afternoon of June 21st. The environment had MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg, effective shear near 35kts, and steep low-level lapse rates. A brief embedded tornado developed within the line and crossed into the Seven Fields, PA region. Model hodographs noted this threat prior to occurrence, indicating that storm motion towards the southeast would be ideal for ingesting streamwise vorticity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media reports trees down and power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two waves of severe storms entered the Ohio River Valley on July 13th. The first, was in the early morning hours. Despite the lack of day time heating, instability was available due to warm, moist advection under southwest flow. Vertical wind shear was also available as a trough approached an area of high pressure and a created a pressure gradient.||During the afternoon, warm and unstable conditions prevailed; dew points stayed in the lower 70s.  Mean-layer CAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg was able to develop.  Due to a gradient between upper high pressure off the east coast and a shortwave trough moving northeast through the Ohio Valley, moderate mid-level flow characterized by 30-40 knots of 0-6km shear prevailed.  This allowed storms to organize into multicellular storm clusters and short line segments.  Many wind damage reports were received, mainly to trees and power lines, although a few reports of structural damage were also noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While a shortwave trough moved south of the Ohio River Valley, isolated to scattered convection initiated in an area of low confluence north of Pittsburgh. As a result, slow moving heavy downpours ignited. Relief from rain didn't come for the city of Butler until outflow boundary collision.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sullivan Run in Butler, PA went out of its banks due to heavy rainfall. In a short amount of time, Butler County EMS and Media reported flooding at the intersection of West Jefferson and West Cunningham Street.  Flooding due to Sullivan Run also occurred at the intersection of West Brady Street and Hayes Ave; additional nearby streets also impacted. Multiple cars stuck and at least one basement was flooded. Pictures suggest at least 1 foot of water over the roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass remained in place over the mid-Atlantic on August 27. A slow moving cold front began dropping southward into the region during the afternoon. This helped scattered to widespread convection develop in a strongly unstable air mass. Shear was weak over the region, but thanks to the strong instability, a number of storms became strong to severe with a threat for damaging downburst winds, in addition to locally heavy rain. Convection continued into the late evening hours before gradually waning in coverage as instability was lost.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed into wires on Molasses Valley Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the Great Lakes region on June 21, while Tropical Storm Claudette passed offshore of New Jersey and the Delmarva region. Ahead of the cold front, a hot, humid, and unstable air mass developed in the mid-Atlantic. A pre-frontal trough then served as a focus for widespread convection across the Northeast and mid-Atlantic during the afternoon and evening hours. With the strong instability and good forcing, much of the convection became strong to severe, taking the form of both linear and semi-discrete activity. Damaging winds were by far the primary hazard with these storms, with numerous reports of downed trees and power lines across the region. Convection weakened as it approached the coast owing to loss of daytime heating, weakening shear, and large scale subsidence on the northwest flank of Claudette.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of downed trees and wires in East Brandywine Twp. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front remained to the north of the region on July 7th. An upper trough dug across the Great Lakes during the daytime hours as well. While better upper-level forcing remained to the north, return flow increased between this trough and the tropical system Elsa to the southeast. An abundant amount of moisture and warm air was thus funneled into our region.  Deep-layer shear was weak, but enough instability and dry air aloft was present to promote many instances of damaging wind gusts.  Damage to trees and power poles was fairly widespread, and there were a few instances of structural damage as well.  Planes were overturned by wind at Butler Airport, and Edgewood Borough declared a local state of emergency due to thunderstorm wind damage. ||Multiple rounds of thunderstorms also lead to flash flooding concerns in the urbanized areas of Allegheny County. Several instances of stuck vehicles were reported, and several homes were evacuated for a time in Bridgeville due to creek flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inch tree limbs were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 16th, a weak positively-tilted trough tracked from the Mississippi River Valley towards the Great Lakes today, while a surface front slowly sagged south from Lake Erie. Diurnal heating in an air mass with rich moisture plus increasing jet support yielded numerous thunderstorms, mainly favoring eastern Ohio and northwest Pennsylvania. Differential heating on the southern fringe of a thicker cloud deck and residual convective outflows provided the catalyst for development. Many reports of tree and power line damage were received. Also, multiple rounds of downpours during the day created flash flooding conditions in portions of Mercer and Venango Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb was brought down on to power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong isolated storm created heavy downpours and tree damage to portions to Carnegie, PA June 8th. At the time of the storm, the region was still under a ridge. Day time heating and deep moisture created enough instability aloft to help thunderstorm development. Based off the radar, it looks like a shortwave over central Ohio, moving east towards I-70, helped make a non-severe storm become stronger. The road that received a flood report is prone to this hazard due to urban runoff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated heavy rain caused Washington Ave near Boden Ave to flood. A car was stuck in high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong storms developed along a trailing cold front on the afternoon of June 21st. The environment had MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg, effective shear near 35kts, and steep low-level lapse rates. A brief embedded tornado developed within the line and crossed into the Seven Fields, PA region. Model hodographs noted this threat prior to occurrence, indicating that storm motion towards the southeast would be ideal for ingesting streamwise vorticity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines down along Bristoria Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass remained in place over the mid-Atlantic on August 27. A slow moving cold front began dropping southward into the region during the afternoon. This helped scattered to widespread convection develop in a strongly unstable air mass. Shear was weak over the region, but thanks to the strong instability, a number of storms became strong to severe with a threat for damaging downburst winds, in addition to locally heavy rain. Convection continued into the late evening hours before gradually waning in coverage as instability was lost.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs and wires were downed west of Reider Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the Great Lakes region on June 21, while Tropical Storm Claudette passed offshore of New Jersey and the Delmarva region. Ahead of the cold front, a hot, humid, and unstable air mass developed in the mid-Atlantic. A pre-frontal trough then served as a focus for widespread convection across the Northeast and mid-Atlantic during the afternoon and evening hours. With the strong instability and good forcing, much of the convection became strong to severe, taking the form of both linear and semi-discrete activity. Damaging winds were by far the primary hazard with these storms, with numerous reports of downed trees and power lines across the region. Convection weakened as it approached the coast owing to loss of daytime heating, weakening shear, and large scale subsidence on the northwest flank of Claudette.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front remained to the north of the region on July 7th. An upper trough dug across the Great Lakes during the daytime hours as well. While better upper-level forcing remained to the north, return flow increased between this trough and the tropical system Elsa to the southeast. An abundant amount of moisture and warm air was thus funneled into our region.  Deep-layer shear was weak, but enough instability and dry air aloft was present to promote many instances of damaging wind gusts.  Damage to trees and power poles was fairly widespread, and there were a few instances of structural damage as well.  Planes were overturned by wind at Butler Airport, and Edgewood Borough declared a local state of emergency due to thunderstorm wind damage. ||Multiple rounds of thunderstorms also lead to flash flooding concerns in the urbanized areas of Allegheny County. Several instances of stuck vehicles were reported, and several homes were evacuated for a time in Bridgeville due to creek flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 16th, a weak positively-tilted trough tracked from the Mississippi River Valley towards the Great Lakes today, while a surface front slowly sagged south from Lake Erie. Diurnal heating in an air mass with rich moisture plus increasing jet support yielded numerous thunderstorms, mainly favoring eastern Ohio and northwest Pennsylvania. Differential heating on the southern fringe of a thicker cloud deck and residual convective outflows provided the catalyst for development. Many reports of tree and power line damage were received. Also, multiple rounds of downpours during the day created flash flooding conditions in portions of Mercer and Venango Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down on North Jackson Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and evening hours of July 20th, a broken line of showers and thunderstorms was able to form along an approaching shortwave trough.  The lack of strong shear kept storms from being too long-lived, but a few instances of wind damage were seen as storms moved west to east to the north of Pittsburgh.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple trees were reported to be down in New Lebanon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass was in place over the mid-Atlantic on August 10. A lee side surface trough was present over central Pennsylvania. This provided sufficient lift for thunderstorms to develop in the unstable air mass, and some organization into multi-cellular or linear clusters occurred. Despite limited shear, the strong instability allowed a number of storms to become strong to severe, producing damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed near the intersection of Bishop Ave and Broadway Ave. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two waves of severe storms entered the Ohio River Valley on July 13th. The first, was in the early morning hours. Despite the lack of day time heating, instability was available due to warm, moist advection under southwest flow. Vertical wind shear was also available as a trough approached an area of high pressure and a created a pressure gradient.||During the afternoon, warm and unstable conditions prevailed; dew points stayed in the lower 70s.  Mean-layer CAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg was able to develop.  Due to a gradient between upper high pressure off the east coast and a shortwave trough moving northeast through the Ohio Valley, moderate mid-level flow characterized by 30-40 knots of 0-6km shear prevailed.  This allowed storms to organize into multicellular storm clusters and short line segments.  Many wind damage reports were received, mainly to trees and power lines, although a few reports of structural damage were also noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were downed on Wible Run Road at Hillwood Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong storms developed along a trailing cold front on the afternoon of June 21st. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two waves of severe storms entered the Ohio River Valley on July 13th. The first, was in the early morning hours. Despite the lack of day time heating, instability was available due to warm, moist advection under southwest flow. Vertical wind shear was also available as a trough approached an area of high pressure and a created a pressure gradient.||During the afternoon, warm and unstable conditions prevailed; dew points stayed in the lower 70s.  Mean-layer CAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg was able to develop.  Due to a gradient between upper high pressure off the east coast and a shortwave trough moving northeast through the Ohio Valley, moderate mid-level flow characterized by 30-40 knots of 0-6km shear prevailed.  This allowed storms to organize into multicellular storm clusters and short line segments.  Many wind damage reports were received, mainly to trees and power lines, although a few reports of structural damage were also noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down on Banksville Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass remained in place over the mid-Atlantic on August 27. A slow moving cold front began dropping southward into the region during the afternoon. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along Frankford Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and associated weak area of surface low pressure approached the mid-Atlantic on June 14. A seasonably warm air mass was present ahead of the front, allowing a moderate amount of instability to build over the mid-Atlantic. Moderate to strong wind shear was also present thanks to the presence of a robust upper level disturbance approaching the region. This disturbance, along with the front, provided sufficient forcing for scattered to widespread convection to develop in the interior mid-Atlantic and propagate towards the coast. Some storms became strong to severe in the rather favorable convective environment, posing a primary risk of damaging winds along with some hail. Convection also continued well into the overnight over portions of the area thanks to the good shear and forcing, with convection eventually moving offshore in the pre-dawn hours of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs were downed near Depot St. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the Great Lakes region on June 21, while Tropical Storm Claudette passed offshore of New Jersey and the Delmarva region. Ahead of the cold front, a hot, humid, and unstable air mass developed in the mid-Atlantic. A pre-frontal trough then served as a focus for widespread convection across the Northeast and mid-Atlantic during the afternoon and evening hours. With the strong instability and good forcing, much of the convection became strong to severe, taking the form of both linear and semi-discrete activity. Damaging winds were by far the primary hazard with these storms, with numerous reports of downed trees and power lines across the region. Convection weakened as it approached the coast owing to loss of daytime heating, weakening shear, and large scale subsidence on the northwest flank of Claudette.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Mill Ln. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very similar to the prior day, a hot and humid air mass was present over the mid-Atlantic on August 11. A lee side surface trough over Pennsylvania again allowed thunderstorms to develop near peak heating. With plenty of instability present and storms taking on some degree of organization, a number of instances of damaging wind occurred as storms moved eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed and blocked Sheephill Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the Great Lakes region on June 21, while Tropical Storm Claudette passed offshore of New Jersey and the Delmarva region. Ahead of the cold front, a hot, humid, and unstable air mass developed in the mid-Atlantic. A pre-frontal trough then served as a focus for widespread convection across the Northeast and mid-Atlantic during the afternoon and evening hours. With the strong instability and good forcing, much of the convection became strong to severe, taking the form of both linear and semi-discrete activity. Damaging winds were by far the primary hazard with these storms, with numerous reports of downed trees and power lines across the region. Convection weakened as it approached the coast owing to loss of daytime heating, weakening shear, and large scale subsidence on the northwest flank of Claudette.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree, wires, and a power pole were downed on a residence near Forge Ln. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and storms developed along a stalled frontal boundary over southern PA during the afternoon and evening hours of July 28, 2021. One of these storms produced multiple wind damage reports as it drifted south-southeastward across York County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph blew the roof off of a structure and knocked wires down in West York.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front remained to the north of the region on July 7th. An upper trough dug across the Great Lakes during the daytime hours as well. While better upper-level forcing remained to the north, return flow increased between this trough and the tropical system Elsa to the southeast. An abundant amount of moisture and warm air was thus funneled into our region.  Deep-layer shear was weak, but enough instability and dry air aloft was present to promote many instances of damaging wind gusts.  Damage to trees and power poles was fairly widespread, and there were a few instances of structural damage as well.  Planes were overturned by wind at Butler Airport, and Edgewood Borough declared a local state of emergency due to thunderstorm wind damage. ||Multiple rounds of thunderstorms also lead to flash flooding concerns in the urbanized areas of Allegheny County. Several instances of stuck vehicles were reported, and several homes were evacuated for a time in Bridgeville due to creek flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass was in place over the mid-Atlantic on August 10. A lee side surface trough was present over central Pennsylvania. This provided sufficient lift for thunderstorms to develop in the unstable air mass, and some organization into multi-cellular or linear clusters occurred. Despite limited shear, the strong instability allowed a number of storms to become strong to severe, producing damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed on Academy Ave near Glen Circle. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass remained in place over the mid-Atlantic on August 27. A slow moving cold front began dropping southward into the region during the afternoon. This helped scattered to widespread convection develop in a strongly unstable air mass. Shear was weak over the region, but thanks to the strong instability, a number of storms became strong to severe with a threat for damaging downburst winds, in addition to locally heavy rain. Convection continued into the late evening hours before gradually waning in coverage as instability was lost.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down around Caln Twp. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two waves of severe storms entered the Ohio River Valley on July 13th. The first, was in the early morning hours. Despite the lack of day time heating, instability was available due to warm, moist advection under southwest flow. Vertical wind shear was also available as a trough approached an area of high pressure and a created a pressure gradient.||During the afternoon, warm and unstable conditions prevailed; dew points stayed in the lower 70s.  Mean-layer CAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg was able to develop.  Due to a gradient between upper high pressure off the east coast and a shortwave trough moving northeast through the Ohio Valley, moderate mid-level flow characterized by 30-40 knots of 0-6km shear prevailed.  This allowed storms to organize into multicellular storm clusters and short line segments.  Many wind damage reports were received, mainly to trees and power lines, although a few reports of structural damage were also noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported to be blown down on Old Mercer Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms formed ahead of a crossing cold front on July 17th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 28th, a weak surface boundary sagged south across western Pennsylvania.  Despite a fair amount of instability, weak upper support, weak shear, and the downmixing of dry air helped to inhibit overall convective coverage.  Nevertheless, a couple of stronger storms were able to develop during the afternoon, creating a few instances of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the intersection of State Route 18 and Halltown Road, the roof of a building was partially torn off, trees were blown down, and large branches were tossed around.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid air mass was in place over the mid-Atlantic on June 8. A weak upper level disturbance helped to initiate thunderstorms over the area during the afternoon hours. Storms were lacking in organization due to weak wind fields and limited forcing, and mainly took the form of single cells and disorganized multi-cells. However, due to the strong to extreme instability present, some of the storms did become strong to severe and produced downbursts containing damaging winds and some hail. Storms also produced torrential rain and areas of flash flooding.Convection waned during the evening hours with the loss of daytime heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downed tree blocked Tennis Ave near Bethlehem Pike. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Fred affected the mid-Atlantic region on the night of August 18 into the predawn hours of August 19. In addition to heavy rain and areas of flooding, Fred's remnants brought a threat for severe weather. As the center of the system moved from central Pennsylvania into the Finger Lakes region of New York, it became increasingly frontal in nature. A warm sector air mass overspread the eastern mid-Atlantic. Instability was limited due to the overnight timing. However, strong shear and forcing combined with tropical moisture allowed for widespread convection to occur as Fred's remnants moved through. Several instances of damaging wind and a couple of tornadoes occurred in the region during the overnight hours, before the system departed on the morning of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down along Fisher Dam Road about 800 feet north |of the intersection with Berne Road. Numerous hardwood tree trunks |were snapped on the western side of the road with a convergent debris|pattern. A corn field on the east side of the road in the same |location was damaged with stalks of corn felled in a convergent |pattern. Further north on Fisher Dam Road, hardwood tree damage was |noted, but it was more sporadic.||Additional damage was noted about a half-mile north of the |intersection of Berne Road and Hill Drive, including numerous trees |snapped on the east side of Berne Road. A little farther north, about|450 feet south of the intersection with Ash Road, a utility pole was|snapped, and hardwood trees were snapped both on the western side |and the eastern side of the road with convergent debris patterns |noted.||There was damage on Ash Road, including a roof that was removed from|a porch and the roof damage extended into the main roof of the |house. The roof was deposited to the east, while at the same time, a |flagpole from the roof supports was tossed to the north. There was |also numerous snapped hardwood trees in this area. Further east on |Ash Road, another house had a large, healthy tree snapped. Tree |damage was also noted on properties on the north side of Ash Road.||Further north of Ash Road, some damage occurred at the Village of |Pleasant Hills Mobile Home community, including hardwood trees |snapped and some cars were damaged by the fallen trees. Some skirting|along the base of a mobile home was mostly torn off. The damage |appeared to become more sporadic and then ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 28th, a weak surface boundary sagged south across western Pennsylvania.  Despite a fair amount of instability, weak upper support, weak shear, and the downmixing of dry air helped to inhibit overall convective coverage.  Nevertheless, a couple of stronger storms were able to develop during the afternoon, creating a few instances of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utility lines were blown down along Ping Ridge Road in Burrell Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong storms developed along a trailing cold front on the afternoon of June 21st. The environment had MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg, effective shear near 35kts, and steep low-level lapse rates. A brief embedded tornado developed within the line and crossed into the Seven Fields, PA region. Model hodographs noted this threat prior to occurrence, indicating that storm motion towards the southeast would be ideal for ingesting streamwise vorticity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass was in place over the mid-Atlantic on August 10. A lee side surface trough was present over central Pennsylvania. This provided sufficient lift for thunderstorms to develop in the unstable air mass, and some organization into multi-cellular or linear clusters occurred. Despite limited shear, the strong instability allowed a number of storms to become strong to severe, producing damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs and other storm debris were downed onto Lancaster Ave at Woodview Dr. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down near the village of Greble to the northwest of Myerstown during the early evening of July 29, 2021. The tornado had estimated peak winds of 80 mph, a path length of just under a three quarters of a mile, and a maximum path width of 60 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado touched down near the village of Greble to the northwest of Myerstown during the early evening of July 29, 2021. The tornado had estimated peak winds of 80 mph, a path length of just under a three quarters of a mile, and a maximum path width of 60 yards. The tornado touched down near Houtztown Road and traveled southeastward before ending near Kutztown Road. A children`s playset was knocked over, along with a few trees being uprooted following a metal gate being destroyed. A portable toilet was thrown about 40 feet right before a row of corn was flattened, clearly showing a convergent wind pattern. Farther southeast, a vehicle was displaced a few feet within a junk yard, prior to the tornado shredding some leaves before lifting just before reaching Kutztown Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two waves of severe storms entered the Ohio River Valley on July 13th. The first, was in the early morning hours. Despite the lack of day time heating, instability was available due to warm, moist advection under southwest flow. Vertical wind shear was also available as a trough approached an area of high pressure and a created a pressure gradient.||During the afternoon, warm and unstable conditions prevailed; dew points stayed in the lower 70s.  Mean-layer CAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg was able to develop.  Due to a gradient between upper high pressure off the east coast and a shortwave trough moving northeast through the Ohio Valley, moderate mid-level flow characterized by 30-40 knots of 0-6km shear prevailed.  This allowed storms to organize into multicellular storm clusters and short line segments.  Many wind damage reports were received, mainly to trees and power lines, although a few reports of structural damage were also noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple trees were blown down near Route 38.
