EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms formed ahead of a crossing cold front on July 17th.  An upper speed max rotating through an upper trough to the west provided support for lift through right entrance region jet dynamics. Sufficient instability and shear were present to allow storms to organize into clusters and line segments. There were scattered instances of tree damage, and also a report of flash flooding in Venango County due to downpours in the moisture-rich environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down in Woodbridgetown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the Great Lakes region on June 21, while Tropical Storm Claudette passed offshore of New Jersey and the Delmarva region. Ahead of the cold front, a hot, humid, and unstable air mass developed in the mid-Atlantic. A pre-frontal trough then served as a focus for widespread convection across the Northeast and mid-Atlantic during the afternoon and evening hours. With the strong instability and good forcing, much of the convection became strong to severe, taking the form of both linear and semi-discrete activity. Damaging winds were by far the primary hazard with these storms, with numerous reports of downed trees and power lines across the region. Convection weakened as it approached the coast owing to loss of daytime heating, weakening shear, and large scale subsidence on the northwest flank of Claudette.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed into wires near the intersection of Miller Rd and Richmond Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid air mass was in place over the mid-Atlantic on June 8. A weak upper level disturbance helped to initiate thunderstorms over the area during the afternoon hours. Storms were lacking in organization due to weak wind fields and limited forcing, and mainly took the form of single cells and disorganized multi-cells. However, due to the strong to extreme instability present, some of the storms did become strong to severe and produced downbursts containing damaging winds and some hail. Storms also produced torrential rain and areas of flash flooding.Convection waned during the evening hours with the loss of daytime heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three trees fell on a vacant vehicle on Dowlin Forge Rd, pulling live wires down on the vehicle. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Fred affected the mid-Atlantic region on the night of August 18 into the predawn hours of August 19. In addition to heavy rain and areas of flooding, Fred's remnants brought a threat for severe weather. As the center of the system moved from central Pennsylvania into the Finger Lakes region of New York, it became increasingly frontal in nature. A warm sector air mass overspread the eastern mid-Atlantic. Instability was limited due to the overnight timing. However, strong shear and forcing combined with tropical moisture allowed for widespread convection to occur as Fred's remnants moved through. Several instances of damaging wind and a couple of tornadoes occurred in the region during the overnight hours, before the system departed on the morning of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After crossing into Bucks County, the tornado then |brought down several trees and branches in the Hidden Springs |mobile home community. Here, several trees were brought down on |homes and across roadways while also leading to minor damage to |the roofs and siding of several residences in the community. The |tornado then proceeded northeastward into Hilltown Township in |Bucks County as it brought down numerous trees in a trajectory |towards Silverdale. Several trees along this line were also |brought down. A metal roof was even torn off of a garage along |Green Street in Silverdale. Several large branches were then |brought down, damaging a roof to a farmhouse along Souderton Road.|The tornado continued its path northeast, bringing down small |trees and snapping the tops of several pine trees at residences |along Woodlawn Drive and Blue School Road. Lastly, the tornado |began to lift as it neared the opposite end of Hilltown Township, |but not before it ripped a section of roofing off an auto repair |shop along North Dublin Pike at the intersection of Blue School |Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front remained to the north of the region on July 7th. An upper trough dug across the Great Lakes during the daytime hours as well. While better upper-level forcing remained to the north, return flow increased between this trough and the tropical system Elsa to the southeast. An abundant amount of moisture and warm air was thus funneled into our region.  Deep-layer shear was weak, but enough instability and dry air aloft was present to promote many instances of damaging wind gusts.  Damage to trees and power poles was fairly widespread, and there were a few instances of structural damage as well.  Planes were overturned by wind at Butler Airport, and Edgewood Borough declared a local state of emergency due to thunderstorm wind damage. ||Multiple rounds of thunderstorms also lead to flash flooding concerns in the urbanized areas of Allegheny County. Several instances of stuck vehicles were reported, and several homes were evacuated for a time in Bridgeville due to creek flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power poles were reported to be blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave passage during the morning of July 29th spread mainly non-severe showers and thunderstorms across the region during the morning and midday hours.  However, a discrete cell along the back edge of the morning activity was able to develop an EF1 tornado along the Fayette-Westmoreland County border. This was a preview of what was to follow. ||In the wake of this morning activity, instability was able to build as partial clearing occurred. Additional thunderstorms were able to develop in association with a prefrontal trough.  These storms were able to take advantage of deep shear, especially in the lower levels.  A cross-boundary component to this shear allowed discrete supercells to form during the mid and late afternoon hours.  Storm motions were favorable for the ingestion of streamwise vorticity, allowing for rotating storms, and the development of additional tornadoes in southwest Pennsylvania and eastern Ohio.  Three additional tornadoes occurred in southwest Pennsylvania, causing generally tree and light structural damage.  Thankfully, no injuries were reported. A few other damaging wind reports were received as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado, that was partially observed by a personal home |surveillance camera and confirmed via damage survey, formed near |Billingsley Road and advanced into an open field before crossing |State Route 417 (near Speer's Public Golf Course) and quickly |dissipating. The tornado passed near a home, snapped about a |dozen pine trees, and then uprooted a shallow rooted hardwood |tree before dissipating. Maximum wind speed was estimated at 85 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass remained in place over the mid-Atlantic on August 27. A slow moving cold front began dropping southward into the region during the afternoon. This helped scattered to widespread convection develop in a strongly unstable air mass. Shear was weak over the region, but thanks to the strong instability, a number of storms became strong to severe with a threat for damaging downburst winds, in addition to locally heavy rain. Convection continued into the late evening hours before gradually waning in coverage as instability was lost.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were downed at Route 30 Bypass and Moore Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and associated weak area of surface low pressure approached the mid-Atlantic on June 14. A seasonably warm air mass was present ahead of the front, allowing a moderate amount of instability to build over the mid-Atlantic. Moderate to strong wind shear was also present thanks to the presence of a robust upper level disturbance approaching the region. This disturbance, along with the front, provided sufficient forcing for scattered to widespread convection to develop in the interior mid-Atlantic and propagate towards the coast. Some storms became strong to severe in the rather favorable convective environment, posing a primary risk of damaging winds along with some hail. Convection also continued well into the overnight over portions of the area thanks to the good shear and forcing, with convection eventually moving offshore in the pre-dawn hours of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Byberry Rd. Time estimated from radar.

While better upper-level forcing remained to the north, return flow increased between this trough and the tropical system Elsa to the southeast. An abundant amount of moisture and warm air was thus funneled into our region.  Deep-layer shear was weak, but enough instability and dry air aloft was present to promote many instances of damaging wind gusts.  Damage to trees and power poles was fairly widespread, and there were a few instances of structural damage as well.  Planes were overturned by wind at Butler Airport, and Edgewood Borough declared a local state of emergency due to thunderstorm wind damage. ||Multiple rounds of thunderstorms also lead to flash flooding concerns in the urbanized areas of Allegheny County. Several instances of stuck vehicles were reported, and several homes were evacuated for a time in Bridgeville due to creek flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snapped trees were reported via social media images on Puckety Church Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave passage during the morning of July 29th spread mainly non-severe showers and thunderstorms across the region during the morning and midday hours.  However, a discrete cell along the back edge of the morning activity was able to develop an EF1 tornado along the Fayette-Westmoreland County border. This was a preview of what was to follow. ||In the wake of this morning activity, instability was able to build as partial clearing occurred. Additional thunderstorms were able to develop in association with a prefrontal trough.  These storms were able to take advantage of deep shear, especially in the lower levels.  A cross-boundary component to this shear allowed discrete supercells to form during the mid and late afternoon hours.  Storm motions were favorable for the ingestion of streamwise vorticity, allowing for rotating storms, and the development of additional tornadoes in southwest Pennsylvania and eastern Ohio.  Three additional tornadoes occurred in southwest Pennsylvania, causing generally tree and light structural damage.  Thankfully, no injuries were reported. A few other damaging wind reports were received as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado, that was observed by live video and confirmed via damage |survey, began in a cornfield near Geesey Road, crossed a farmers property, |and dissipated at the end of the property. Minor crop damage was noted|near Geesey Road where the tornado began. The tornado then tracked |east-southeast where it struck the side of a barn and tossed portions |of the roofing and siding 100 feet away from the structure. Finally, |some damage was noted on the side of a open barn shed where a 2x4 struck a |metal side of a structure. Maximum wind speed was estimated at 75 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong storms developed along a trailing cold front on the afternoon of June 21st. The environment had MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg, effective shear near 35kts, and steep low-level lapse rates. A brief embedded tornado developed within the line and crossed into the Seven Fields, PA region. Model hodographs noted this threat prior to occurrence, indicating that storm motion towards the southeast would be ideal for ingesting streamwise vorticity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on Walker Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two waves of severe storms entered the Ohio River Valley on July 13th. The first, was in the early morning hours. Despite the lack of day time heating, instability was available due to warm, moist advection under southwest flow. Vertical wind shear was also available as a trough approached an area of high pressure and a created a pressure gradient.||During the afternoon, warm and unstable conditions prevailed; dew points stayed in the lower 70s.  Mean-layer CAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg was able to develop.  Due to a gradient between upper high pressure off the east coast and a shortwave trough moving northeast through the Ohio Valley, moderate mid-level flow characterized by 30-40 knots of 0-6km shear prevailed.  This allowed storms to organize into multicellular storm clusters and short line segments.  Many wind damage reports were received, mainly to trees and power lines, although a few reports of structural damage were also noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Wood Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the Great Lakes region on June 21, while Tropical Storm Claudette passed offshore of New Jersey and the Delmarva region. Ahead of the cold front, a hot, humid, and unstable air mass developed in the mid-Atlantic. A pre-frontal trough then served as a focus for widespread convection across the Northeast and mid-Atlantic during the afternoon and evening hours. With the strong instability and good forcing, much of the convection became strong to severe, taking the form of both linear and semi-discrete activity. Damaging winds were by far the primary hazard with these storms, with numerous reports of downed trees and power lines across the region. Convection weakened as it approached the coast owing to loss of daytime heating, weakening shear, and large scale subsidence on the northwest flank of Claudette.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed near Grant St. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and associated weak area of surface low pressure approached the mid-Atlantic on June 14. A seasonably warm air mass was present ahead of the front, allowing a moderate amount of instability to build over the mid-Atlantic. Moderate to strong wind shear was also present thanks to the presence of a robust upper level disturbance approaching the region. This disturbance, along with the front, provided sufficient forcing for scattered to widespread convection to develop in the interior mid-Atlantic and propagate towards the coast. Some storms became strong to severe in the rather favorable convective environment, posing a primary risk of damaging winds along with some hail. Convection also continued well into the overnight over portions of the area thanks to the good shear and forcing, with convection eventually moving offshore in the pre-dawn hours of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An mPing report of large tree limbs or power lines down. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid air mass was in place over the mid-Atlantic on June 8. A weak upper level disturbance helped to initiate thunderstorms over the area during the afternoon hours. Storms were lacking in organization due to weak wind fields and limited forcing, and mainly took the form of single cells and disorganized multi-cells. However, due to the strong to extreme instability present, some of the storms did become strong to severe and produced downbursts containing damaging winds and some hail. Storms also produced torrential rain and areas of flash flooding.Convection waned during the evening hours with the loss of daytime heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house on Somerset Dr. The local fire department responded for a smell of smoke in the basement. Time estimated from radar and dispatch report.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado damaged a rural portion of extreme western Greene|County during the early evening of July 29th. The affected area |was just south of State Gamelands No. 302, in a region of |rough terrain characterized by dense wooded areas, mixed with open|farm land and gas well production infrastructure.||Damage from this tornado was confined to mainly hardwood trees in|the wooded areas. The tornado snapped a number of small trees, |uprooted several larger ones, and downed numerous large branches |over the course of its nearly 3 mile track. The initial damage |occurred at the bridge crossing Enlow Fork on the Pennsylvania |side of the MarkWest Majorsville Station. As the storm tracked to |the southeast, it reached peak intensity along Day Road, where |considerable tree damage was noted from an adjacent hill, |extending down into a deep ravine. Several large trees were |uprooted along with many smaller ones snapped. The tornado's maximum wind speed of 105 MPH was estimated in this area. Sporadic tree damage was noted as the tornado moved southeast and crossed Fry Hill Road, Scott Hill Road, and finally along the southern spur of |Day Road once again (just southeast of the Veterans Road fork).||This is the eighth documented tornado to impact Greene County |since 1950, and is the first since October 2nd 2018. In addition, |this marks the 8th tornado confirmed within the NWS Pittsburgh |forecast area from the July 29th outbreak, the most in a single |event since May 1st 2017, when seven tornadoes were recorded|over northwest Pennsylvania.

Moderate to strong wind shear was also present thanks to the presence of a robust upper level disturbance approaching the region. This disturbance, along with the front, provided sufficient forcing for scattered to widespread convection to develop in the interior mid-Atlantic and propagate towards the coast. Some storms became strong to severe in the rather favorable convective environment, posing a primary risk of damaging winds along with some hail. Convection also continued well into the overnight over portions of the area thanks to the good shear and forcing, with convection eventually moving offshore in the pre-dawn hours of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed on Montgomery Ave. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the Great Lakes region on June 21, while Tropical Storm Claudette passed offshore of New Jersey and the Delmarva region. Ahead of the cold front, a hot, humid, and unstable air mass developed in the mid-Atlantic. A pre-frontal trough then served as a focus for widespread convection across the Northeast and mid-Atlantic during the afternoon and evening hours. With the strong instability and good forcing, much of the convection became strong to severe, taking the form of both linear and semi-discrete activity. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Valley Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe thunderstorms formed during the afternoon and evening within an environment of moderate CAPE and moderate shear. A shortwave moved across the Ohio Valley giving support for multiple rounds of convection, primarily in the form of multicell clusters and linear segments. Although damaging winds were the main impact, a few storms also created large hail.  Flash flooding was reported from slow moving thunderstorms in Lycoming and Tioga Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a lightning strike that caused a house fire in the 200 block of Brothers Court in Patton Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and storms developed along a stalled frontal boundary over southern PA during the afternoon and evening hours of July 28, 2021. One of these storms produced multiple wind damage reports as it drifted south-southeastward across York County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced winds estimated near 60 mph and knocked down several tents at the York State Fair. There were 6 injuries, with 3 of them transported to the hospital for further evaluation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the Susquehanna Valley during the early evening hours of July 9, 2021, accompanied by scattered thunderstorms. There were wind damage reports along the path of a particularly impressive bow echo that developed over eastern Perry County and dropped southeastward across Dauphin, Lancaster and far southwestern Lebanon counties.  Further north, a band of heavy rain remained nearly stationary during the afternoon, producing flooding in the Mansfield area of Tioga County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a tree onto wires on Mount Pleasant Road near Dauphin Boro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very similar to the prior day, a hot and humid air mass was present over the mid-Atlantic on August 11. A lee side surface trough over Pennsylvania again allowed thunderstorms to develop near peak heating. With plenty of instability present and storms taking on some degree of organization, a number of instances of damaging wind occurred as storms moved eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were a few reports of power outages due to tree damage in the Dublin area, including near Irish Meetinghouse Rd. Likely associated with outflow winds. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front remained to the north of the region on July 7th. An upper trough dug across the Great Lakes during the daytime hours as well. While better upper-level forcing remained to the north, return flow increased between this trough and the tropical system Elsa to the southeast. An abundant amount of moisture and warm air was thus funneled into our region.  Deep-layer shear was weak, but enough instability and dry air aloft was present to promote many instances of damaging wind gusts.  Damage to trees and power poles was fairly widespread, and there were a few instances of structural damage as well.  Planes were overturned by wind at Butler Airport, and Edgewood Borough declared a local state of emergency due to thunderstorm wind damage. ||Multiple rounds of thunderstorms also lead to flash flooding concerns in the urbanized areas of Allegheny County. Several instances of stuck vehicles were reported, and several homes were evacuated for a time in Bridgeville due to creek flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave passage during the morning of July 29th spread mainly non-severe showers and thunderstorms across the region during the morning and midday hours.  However, a discrete cell along the back edge of the morning activity was able to develop an EF1 tornado along the Fayette-Westmoreland County border. This was a preview of what was to follow. ||In the wake of this morning activity, instability was able to build as partial clearing occurred. Additional thunderstorms were able to develop in association with a prefrontal trough.  These storms were able to take advantage of deep shear, especially in the lower levels.  A cross-boundary component to this shear allowed discrete supercells to form during the mid and late afternoon hours.  Storm motions were favorable for the ingestion of streamwise vorticity, allowing for rotating storms, and the development of additional tornadoes in southwest Pennsylvania and eastern Ohio.  Three additional tornadoes occurred in southwest Pennsylvania, causing generally tree and light structural damage.  Thankfully, no injuries were reported. A few other damaging wind reports were received as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Initial damage was noted on Bear Rocks Road near Grandview Road,|consisting of large branch snaps and uprooted trees consistent with|EF0 damage. The tornado then continued roughly along Grandview Road|towards Mount Zion Road with similar additional tree damage.||The tornado then crossed Mount Zion Church Road and gained strength,|causing minor damage to a roof and a deck.  The tornado crossed a field|and snapped a few apple trees, and then caused minor damage to a barn.|Near this barn, a tree trunk was snapped, consistent with EF-1 wind|speed. ||The most intense damage was noted on Kessler School Road. A manufactured|shed was tossed on its side about 20 feet.  Also, a manufactured garage|had partial wall collapse and a roof blown off, with the roof transported about 20 feet downwind.  EF1 damage of 100 to 105 MPH (the maximum for the tornado) was assigned here.  Additional tree damage occurred further down Kessler School Road to the intersection with Coffman Road.  A storage building was tipped over on its side, but the roof was intact and the structure was sitting unattached on cinder blocks, consistent with 90 to 95 MPH EF0 wind. The tornado then crossed a hill, moving into Westmoreland County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms developed along an approaching front on Monday, June 14th. Strong shear (60kts in the 0-6km layer), moderate instability (CAPE of 1000-1500 J/kg), and dry mid-level air helped produce strong downdrafts and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass remained in place over the mid-Atlantic on August 27. A slow moving cold front began dropping southward into the region during the afternoon. This helped scattered to widespread convection develop in a strongly unstable air mass. Shear was weak over the region, but thanks to the strong instability, a number of storms became strong to severe with a threat for damaging downburst winds, in addition to locally heavy rain. Convection continued into the late evening hours before gradually waning in coverage as instability was lost.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed near Maple Dr. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two waves of severe storms entered the Ohio River Valley on July 13th. The first, was in the early morning hours. Despite the lack of day time heating, instability was available due to warm, moist advection under southwest flow. Vertical wind shear was also available as a trough approached an area of high pressure and a created a pressure gradient.||During the afternoon, warm and unstable conditions prevailed; dew points stayed in the lower 70s.  Mean-layer CAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg was able to develop.  Due to a gradient between upper high pressure off the east coast and a shortwave trough moving northeast through the Ohio Valley, moderate mid-level flow characterized by 30-40 knots of 0-6km shear prevailed.  This allowed storms to organize into multicellular storm clusters and short line segments.  Many wind damage reports were received, mainly to trees and power lines, although a few reports of structural damage were also noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A number of trees were reported to be blown down in the northeastern portion of Indiana County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Fred affected the mid-Atlantic region on the night of August 18 into the predawn hours of August 19. In addition to heavy rain and areas of flooding, Fred's remnants brought a threat for severe weather. As the center of the system moved from central Pennsylvania into the Finger Lakes region of New York, it became increasingly frontal in nature. A warm sector air mass overspread the eastern mid-Atlantic. Instability was limited due to the overnight timing. However, strong shear and forcing combined with tropical moisture allowed for widespread convection to occur as Fred's remnants moved through. Several instances of damaging wind and a couple of tornadoes occurred in the region during the overnight hours, before the system departed on the morning of the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Fred affected the mid-Atlantic region on the night of August 18 into the predawn hours of August 19. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across Montgomery and Bucks Counties |early on the morning of August 19th as the remnants of Tropical Storm|Fred moved across central Pennsylvania. As a velocity couplet formed across Montgomery County, a tornado |formed roughly a mile and a half south of Souderton, PA around |12:25 AM EDT, where it brought down a tree branch and tore siding |off of a residence on Garrison Lane. From there, the tornado |appears to have lifted briefly before bringing down a few small |trees and branches along Township Line Road, near County Line Rd at Bethlehem Pike. The tornado then moved into Bucks County, where its path continued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and unstable air mass was in place over the mid-Atlantic on August 26. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong storms developed along a trailing cold front on the afternoon of June 21st. The environment had MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg, effective shear near 35kts, and steep low-level lapse rates. A brief embedded tornado developed within the line and crossed into the Seven Fields, PA region. Model hodographs noted this threat prior to occurrence, indicating that storm motion towards the southeast would be ideal for ingesting streamwise vorticity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media reports trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front remained to the north of the region on July 7th. An upper trough dug across the Great Lakes during the daytime hours as well. While better upper-level forcing remained to the north, return flow increased between this trough and the tropical system Elsa to the southeast. An abundant amount of moisture and warm air was thus funneled into our region.  Deep-layer shear was weak, but enough instability and dry air aloft was present to promote many instances of damaging wind gusts.  Damage to trees and power poles was fairly widespread, and there were a few instances of structural damage as well.  Planes were overturned by wind at Butler Airport, and Edgewood Borough declared a local state of emergency due to thunderstorm wind damage. ||Multiple rounds of thunderstorms also lead to flash flooding concerns in the urbanized areas of Allegheny County. Several instances of stuck vehicles were reported, and several homes were evacuated for a time in Bridgeville due to creek flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pinewood and Lakeview Drive were both closed due to flooding, with over 2 feet of running water at the intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave passage during the morning of July 29th spread mainly non-severe showers and thunderstorms across the region during the morning and midday hours.  However, a discrete cell along the back edge of the morning activity was able to develop an EF1 tornado along the Fayette-Westmoreland County border. This was a preview of what was to follow. ||In the wake of this morning activity, instability was able to build as partial clearing occurred. Additional thunderstorms were able to develop in association with a prefrontal trough.  These storms were able to take advantage of deep shear, especially in the lower levels.  A cross-boundary component to this shear allowed discrete supercells to form during the mid and late afternoon hours.  Storm motions were favorable for the ingestion of streamwise vorticity, allowing for rotating storms, and the development of additional tornadoes in southwest Pennsylvania and eastern Ohio.  Three additional tornadoes occurred in southwest Pennsylvania, causing generally tree and light structural damage.  Thankfully, no injuries were reported. A few other damaging wind reports were received as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After crossing the Fayette/Westmoreland County line, the tornado continued across a hill. It crossed County Line Road and Hellen School Road, and it possibly lifted briefly as no damage was noted during the survey in this area. The last noted damage occurred on Mountain View Road, with snapped tree branches and damage to a tin roof at Mountain View Acres. The tornado then dissipated in the woods along Mount Olive Road. Although the tornado was rated EF-1 based on damage in Fayette County, the maximum wind speed for the Westmoreland portion of the track was 60 to 70 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the Great Lakes region on June 21, while Tropical Storm Claudette passed offshore of New Jersey and the Delmarva region. Ahead of the cold front, a hot, humid, and unstable air mass developed in the mid-Atlantic. A pre-frontal trough then served as a focus for widespread convection across the Northeast and mid-Atlantic during the afternoon and evening hours. With the strong instability and good forcing, much of the convection became strong to severe, taking the form of both linear and semi-discrete activity. Damaging winds were by far the primary hazard with these storms, with numerous reports of downed trees and power lines across the region. Convection weakened as it approached the coast owing to loss of daytime heating, weakening shear, and large scale subsidence on the northwest flank of Claudette.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two waves of severe storms entered the Ohio River Valley on July 13th. The first, was in the early morning hours. Despite the lack of day time heating, instability was available due to warm, moist advection under southwest flow. Vertical wind shear was also available as a trough approached an area of high pressure and a created a pressure gradient.||During the afternoon, warm and unstable conditions prevailed; dew points stayed in the lower 70s.  Mean-layer CAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg was able to develop.  Due to a gradient between upper high pressure off the east coast and a shortwave trough moving northeast through the Ohio Valley, moderate mid-level flow characterized by 30-40 knots of 0-6km shear prevailed.  This allowed storms to organize into multicellular storm clusters and short line segments.  Many wind damage reports were received, mainly to trees and power lines, although a few reports of structural damage were also noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind threw bricks on to a roof, blew a hot tub cover through a sliding glass door, and knocked down numerous trees along Stiffler Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and associated weak area of surface low pressure approached the mid-Atlantic on June 14. A seasonably warm air mass was present ahead of the front, allowing a moderate amount of instability to build over the mid-Atlantic. Moderate to strong wind shear was also present thanks to the presence of a robust upper level disturbance approaching the region. This disturbance, along with the front, provided sufficient forcing for scattered to widespread convection to develop in the interior mid-Atlantic and propagate towards the coast. Some storms became strong to severe in the rather favorable convective environment, posing a primary risk of damaging winds along with some hail. Convection also continued well into the overnight over portions of the area thanks to the good shear and forcing, with convection eventually moving offshore in the pre-dawn hours of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An mPing report of penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the Susquehanna Valley during the early evening hours of July 9, 2021, accompanied by scattered thunderstorms. There were wind damage reports along the path of a particularly impressive bow echo that developed over eastern Perry County and dropped southeastward across Dauphin, Lancaster and far southwestern Lebanon counties.  Further north, a band of heavy rain remained nearly stationary during the afternoon, producing flooding in the Mansfield area of Tioga County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a tree onto a home near Hershey Drive and East Adelle Avenue in Manheim.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two waves of severe storms entered the Ohio River Valley on July 13th. The first, was in the early morning hours. Despite the lack of day time heating, instability was available due to warm, moist advection under southwest flow. Vertical wind shear was also available as a trough approached an area of high pressure and a created a pressure gradient.||During the afternoon, warm and unstable conditions prevailed; dew points stayed in the lower 70s.  Mean-layer CAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg was able to develop.  Due to a gradient between upper high pressure off the east coast and a shortwave trough moving northeast through the Ohio Valley, moderate mid-level flow characterized by 30-40 knots of 0-6km shear prevailed.  This allowed storms to organize into multicellular storm clusters and short line segments.  Many wind damage reports were received, mainly to trees and power lines, although a few reports of structural damage were also noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Centerville Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the Great Lakes region on June 21, while Tropical Storm Claudette passed offshore of New Jersey and the Delmarva region. Ahead of the cold front, a hot, humid, and unstable air mass developed in the mid-Atlantic. A pre-frontal trough then served as a focus for widespread convection across the Northeast and mid-Atlantic during the afternoon and evening hours. With the strong instability and good forcing, much of the convection became strong to severe, taking the form of both linear and semi-discrete activity. Damaging winds were by far the primary hazard with these storms, with numerous reports of downed trees and power lines across the region. Convection weakened as it approached the coast owing to loss of daytime heating, weakening shear, and large scale subsidence on the northwest flank of Claudette.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outflow winds downed trees and wires near Hill Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the Susquehanna Valley during the early evening hours of July 9, 2021, accompanied by scattered thunderstorms. There were wind damage reports along the path of a particularly impressive bow echo that developed over eastern Perry County and dropped southeastward across Dauphin, Lancaster and far southwestern Lebanon counties.  Further north, a band of heavy rain remained nearly stationary during the afternoon, producing flooding in the Mansfield area of Tioga County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a tree onto an occupied vehicle on Fruitville Pike between Lititz Road and Erbs Quarry Road. There were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very similar to the prior day, a hot and humid air mass was present over the mid-Atlantic on August 11. A lee side surface trough over Pennsylvania again allowed thunderstorms to develop near peak heating. With plenty of instability present and storms taking on some degree of organization, a number of instances of damaging wind occurred as storms moved eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed onto wires on Jefferson Ave in Spring Twp. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong storms developed along a trailing cold front on the afternoon of June 21st. The environment had MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg, effective shear near 35kts, and steep low-level lapse rates. A brief embedded tornado developed within the line and crossed into the Seven Fields, PA region. Model hodographs noted this threat prior to occurrence, indicating that storm motion towards the southeast would be ideal for ingesting streamwise vorticity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire Dept reported large tree down on wires on Depot Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass was in place over the mid-Atlantic on August 10. A lee side surface trough was present over central Pennsylvania. This provided sufficient lift for thunderstorms to develop in the unstable air mass, and some organization into multi-cellular or linear clusters occurred. Despite limited shear, the strong instability allowed a number of storms to become strong to severe, producing damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed on Susan Ln near Arlington Ave. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front remained to the north of the region on July 7th. An upper trough dug across the Great Lakes during the daytime hours as well. While better upper-level forcing remained to the north, return flow increased between this trough and the tropical system Elsa to the southeast. An abundant amount of moisture and warm air was thus funneled into our region.  Deep-layer shear was weak, but enough instability and dry air aloft was present to promote many instances of damaging wind gusts.  Damage to trees and power poles was fairly widespread, and there were a few instances of structural damage as well.  Planes were overturned by wind at Butler Airport, and Edgewood Borough declared a local state of emergency due to thunderstorm wind damage. ||Multiple rounds of thunderstorms also lead to flash flooding concerns in the urbanized areas of Allegheny County. Several instances of stuck vehicles were reported, and several homes were evacuated for a time in Bridgeville due to creek flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle was stuck in rising water on Station Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and moist tropical air was present over Northeastern Pennsylvania when a quasi-stationary warm front slowly moved through the region. These conditions provided support for one storm to become severe with damaging winds, knocking down a tree onto a roadway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto a roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing shortwave across eastern Ohio sparked the development of showers and storms. Effective wind shear was weak but DCAPE ranged from 800-900 J/kg just before storms moved through; PWATS also ranged between 1.7 to 1.9 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local 911 Call Center reported trees and wires down on Shadow Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of mesoscale convective systems over the Great Lakes advanced into the Ohio River Valley throughout the day on August 12th and helped to create severe storms. Despite little effective shear, DCAPE was very high (between 1300-1400J/kg) and was enough to accelerate downdrafts, causing strong surface wind. Weak zonal flow also created an ideal situation for training storms to develop as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding reported by local 911 Call Center; flooding on Turner Valley Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary, coupled with an extremely warm and humid environment, produced another day of slow moving, torrential downpour producing thunderstorms over Northeast Pennsylvania. Stripes of 1 to 3 inch rainfall occurred over several parts of the area. The greatest rain totals occurred over a large part of Bradford county where 3 to 5 inches accumulated in just a few ours. The region was again hit with major impacts from flash flooding of local creeks, streams and overwhelmed storm drainage systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 220 was flooded and possibly washed away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through Northeastern Pennsylvania with multiple storms forming along it. Many storms became severe due to instability ahead of the front, resulting in multiple reports of trees and wires down. Lightning struck a house, resulting in the house catching on fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a large tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through Northeastern Pennsylvania with multiple storms forming along it. Many storms became severe due to instability ahead of the front, resulting in multiple reports of trees and wires down. Lightning struck a house, resulting in the house catching on fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A residence was struck by lightning and caught on fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of showers and isolated thunderstorms developed over central Pennsylvania during the afternoon of September 9, 2021, ahead of a secondary cold front crossing the region. One of these storms produced wind damage in western Franklin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 50 mph pulled some metal sheathing off of a roof on an out-building in Lemasters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of mesoscale convective systems over the Great Lakes advanced into the Ohio River Valley throughout the day on August 12th and helped to create severe storms. Despite little effective shear, DCAPE was very high (between 1300-1400J/kg) and was enough to accelerate downdrafts, causing strong surface wind. Weak zonal flow also created an ideal situation for training storms to develop as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local 911 call center reported multiple trees down on Hamilton Porter Rd and Ewing Rd in Perry Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing shortwave across eastern Ohio sparked the development of showers and storms. Effective wind shear was weak but DCAPE ranged from 800-900 J/kg just before storms moved through; PWATS also ranged between 1.7 to 1.9 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a tree down near Bakerstown Road and Windfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed the afternoon of the 13th as a result of a passing shortwave to the south and a slow moving trough located north of the Great Lakes. Just like the previous day, despite the fact that effective shear was slightly weak, DCAPE was ranging between 900 to 1000 J/kg. Also the ground was well saturated from storms the previous day, therefore a number of isolated areas were vulnerable to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy downpours along a frontal boundary combined with eastward-moving showers and storms, which developed south of Columbus, OH, to create flash flooding and areal flooding south of Pittsburgh.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first report of flooding was from Venetia, indicating that Sugar Camp Road was flooded.  Flooding along Bunola River Road/Glassport-Elizabeth/Second Ave/Center Ave/Lincoln Blvd was reported by the Highway Department near Elizabeth, PA. Near Industry, Lowber Road between Carnegie Road and Kangaroo Hill Road was closed due to flooding; emergency crews reported about a foot of water running across the road. Monongahela Fire Department was partially flooded in the city of Monongahela. A few roads in the city of Washington, Houston, and Canonsburg also had flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spotty lake-effect and upslope snow showers dusted the western highlands of Pennsylvania with snow during the early morning hours of November 23, 2021. Resultant slick roads in Clearfield County led to an accident that closed the westbound lanes of I-80 for two hours near mile marker 120. There were no injuries reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers produced slick road conditions on I-80 westbound near mile marker 120 in Clearfield County, resulting an accident that prompted the closing of the westbound lanes for two hours. There were no injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire burned 337 acres in in Glade Township, Warren County from April 8-13, 2021. Rain showers during the early morning hours of April 13 aided firefighters in the containment of the wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire burned 337 acres in the vicinity of 3735 Hemlock Road in Glade Township, Warren County from April 8-13, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Fred affected the mid-Atlantic region on the night of August 18 into the predawn hours of August 19. In addition to heavy rain and areas of flooding, Fred's remnants brought a threat for severe weather. As the center of the system moved from central Pennsylvania into the Finger Lakes region of New York, it became increasingly frontal in nature. A warm sector air mass overspread the eastern mid-Atlantic. Instability was limited due to the overnight timing. However, strong shear and forcing combined with tropical moisture allowed for widespread convection to occur as Fred's remnants moved through. Several instances of damaging wind and a couple of tornadoes occurred in the region during the overnight hours, before the system departed on the morning of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of trees down between Malvern and Phoenixville. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary, coupled with an extremely warm and humid environment, produced another day of slow moving, torrential downpour producing thunderstorms over Northeast Pennsylvania. Stripes of 1 to 3 inch rainfall occurred over several parts of the area. The greatest rain totals occurred over a large part of Bradford county where 3 to 5 inches accumulated in just a few ours. The region was again hit with major impacts from flash flooding of local creeks, streams and overwhelmed storm drainage systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads, along with a few houses were flooded between Ridgebury and Columbia Crossroads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Fred affected the mid-Atlantic region on the night of August 18 into the predawn hours of August 19. In addition to heavy rain and areas of flooding, Fred's remnants brought a threat for severe weather. As the center of the system moved from central Pennsylvania into the Finger Lakes region of New York, it became increasingly frontal in nature. A warm sector air mass overspread the eastern mid-Atlantic. Instability was limited due to the overnight timing. However, strong shear and forcing combined with tropical moisture allowed for widespread convection to occur as Fred's remnants moved through. Several instances of damaging wind and a couple of tornadoes occurred in the region during the overnight hours, before the system departed on the morning of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of hail up to penny size in the area. While unremarkable on its own, this was considered highly unusual and surprising, as hail is rarely observed with tropical cyclone remnants.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary, coupled with an extremely warm and humid environment, produced another day of slow moving, torrential downpour producing thunderstorms over Northeast Pennsylvania. Stripes of 1 to 3 inch rainfall occurred over several parts of the area. The greatest rain totals occurred over a large part of Bradford county where 3 to 5 inches accumulated in just a few ours. The region was again hit with major impacts from flash flooding of local creeks, streams and overwhelmed storm drainage systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding had caused a mudslide across Route 220 to the south of Ulster.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of mesoscale convective systems over the Great Lakes advanced into the Ohio River Valley throughout the day on August 12th and helped to create severe storms. Despite little effective shear, DCAPE was very high (between 1300-1400J/kg) and was enough to accelerate downdrafts, causing strong surface wind. Weak zonal flow also created an ideal situation for training storms to develop as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported large tree branches down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Fred affected the mid-Atlantic region on the night of August 18 into the predawn hours of August 19. In addition to heavy rain and areas of flooding, Fred's remnants brought a threat for severe weather. As the center of the system moved from central Pennsylvania into the Finger Lakes region of New York, it became increasingly frontal in nature. A warm sector air mass overspread the eastern mid-Atlantic. Instability was limited due to the overnight timing. However, strong shear and forcing combined with tropical moisture allowed for widespread convection to occur as Fred's remnants moved through. Several instances of damaging wind and a couple of tornadoes occurred in the region during the overnight hours, before the system departed on the morning of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of tree and power line damage in the Downingtown, West Chester, and Exton areas, with several roads closed. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front during the afternoon of the 6th across portions of Northeast Pennsylvania. A few of these storms became severe with damaging winds. Mainly tree and powerline damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved through Northeast Pennsylvania, triggering scattered thunderstorms. Several storms produced torrential rainfall which led to areas of flash flooding. A narrow band of rainfall totaling 2 to 4 inches stretched across the northeast quarter of the county. This rain fell in under 4 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bridge on Merry Go Round Road was washed out as a result of torrential rainfall and flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lingering mesoscale convective vortex over northwest Ohio that is acting as a trigger for developing thunderstorms on its eastern flank. 850-300mb mean steering flow, suggested at storm motion had the potential to slow down and create flash flooding in regions downstream (i.e. western PA).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooded roadways north of Butler, roads included: Hall Road, Mahood Road, Route 38, and Brady Street near Miler Avenue in the city of Butler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak flow aloft and high PWATs (1.77 at PIT), which was just below daily records but in the upper 1-2% of observations at this time of year, created the ideal environment for flash flooding to occur in isolated regions of western PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple areas experienced flooded due to back building storm. Richey Run broke from its banks and flooded Route 208, Route 338 was closed between Ritts Station Road to Blairs Corners Road, and roadways near the county line was affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving Alberta Clipper spread a swath of light snow across much of central Pennsylvania from the evening of November 27 into the early morning hours of November 28, 2021. One to three inches of accumulation were observed in locations north and west of the Lower Susquehanna Valley. This was enough to slicken roads, and Route 15 was closed in South Williamsport due to multiple accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Alberta Clipper produced 1 to 2 inches of light snow and slickened roads across southern Lycoming County. Resultant slick roads caused Route 15 to be closed in both directions over the mountain between Fairmont Avenue in South Williamsport and Route 54 in Clinton Township due to multiple accidents, including a jackknifed tractor trailer. The road was closed for a little more than two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure in the Gulf of Maine dragged a strong cold front across central Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of April 30, 2021. A secondary cold front crossed the area during the day on April 30. These systems produced strong winds across the area, particularly across southeastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were estimated to have gusted to near 60 mph in Adams County on April 30, 2021. These winds knocked down a tree onto an occupied porta-potty at Gettysburg National Battlefield, entrapping and causing minor injuries to the occupant.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were estimated to have gusted to near 60 mph in Cumberland County on April 30, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass remained in place over the mid-Atlantic on August 27. A slow moving cold front began dropping southward into the region during the afternoon. This helped scattered to widespread convection develop in a strongly unstable air mass. Shear was weak over the region, but thanks to the strong instability, a number of storms became strong to severe with a threat for damaging downburst winds, in addition to locally heavy rain. Convection continued into the late evening hours before gradually waning in coverage as instability was lost.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed near Plum Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain associated with Post-Tropical Cyclone Fred caused flash flooding in eastern Pennsylvania on the night of August 18-19, 2021.  There were rainfall totals as high as 3 to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three water rescues took place in Paoli, one at the intersection of East Lancaster Avenue and Leopard Road, another at the intersection of Maple Avenue and Agnew Lane, and a final one at the intersection of Paoli Pike and Rustic Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An advancing cold front, along with shortwave support, created the chance for thunderstorms across the region. Sufficient shear (40kts in the 0-6km layer), moderate to high instability (1500-2000 J/kg of CAPE), and dry mid-level air resulted in damaging winds and hail. Flash flooding occurred, mostly south of Pittsburgh, with training storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Less than quarter sized hail fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms developed along an approaching front on Monday, June 14th. Strong shear (60kts in the 0-6km layer), moderate instability (CAPE of 1000-1500 J/kg), and dry mid-level air helped produce strong downdrafts and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A near record warm air mass was in place over the mid-Atlantic on June 30. Combined with fairly high humidity, moderate to strong instability built over the region. An approaching surface trough acted to trigger scattered thunderstorms over the area during the afternoon and evening hours. Shear was not very strong, which limited overall storm organization. However, some storms did become strong to severe and produced instances of damaging winds. Convective activity diminished during the later evening hours with the loss of daytime heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of downed trees and wires near North Ridge Rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level trough with strong mid-level flow and diurnal heating under a moist low-level airmass helped generate severe storms across the Ohio River Valley. Effective bulk shear was 30-40kts and supported storm organization. ML CAPE ranged from 1000-2000J/kg by mid-afternoon, steep lapse rates helped efficient downdraft acceleration.  ||Training storms also created localized flooding along a convergence axis from southeast Ohio to southwest PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 1 inch was observed by a citizen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very similar to the prior day, a hot and humid air mass was present over the mid-Atlantic on August 11. With plenty of instability present and storms taking on some degree of organization, a number of instances of damaging wind occurred as storms moved eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong storms developed along a trailing cold front on the afternoon of June 21st. The environment had MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg, effective shear near 35kts, and steep low-level lapse rates. A brief embedded tornado developed within the line and crossed into the Seven Fields, PA region. Model hodographs noted this threat prior to occurrence, indicating that storm motion towards the southeast would be ideal for ingesting streamwise vorticity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and wires down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong isolated storm created heavy downpours and tree damage to portions to Carnegie, PA June 8th. At the time of the storm, the region was still under a ridge. Day time heating and deep moisture created enough instability aloft to help thunderstorm development. Based off the radar, it looks like a shortwave over central Ohio, moving east towards I-70, helped make a non-severe storm become stronger. The road that received a flood report is prone to this hazard due to urban runoff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell down on an occupied car on Chestnut Street. One female received minor cuts and abrasions from broken glass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of showers and a few thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front that was approaching central Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening of September 8, 2021. One particular cell along this line intensified over Mifflin County and produced sporadic wind damage as it progressed eastward across Snyder and Northumberland counties. This cell also produced two weak tornadoes (an EF-0 and an EF-1) in Snyder County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down several trees along Route 104 near Mount Pleasant Mills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest PA remained in the warm sector during the evening of June 20th through the afternoon of June 21st. Simultaneously, a low moved northeastward from the Upper Mississippi Valley to west-central QC and dragged a cold front eastward toward northwest PA. A cluster of multicell thunderstorms moved east-northeastward through the Erie area and produced straight-line wind damage during the late evening of the 20th. During the early afternoon of the 21st, a cluster of multicells and a supercell moved eastward across Crawford County and produced additional straight-line wind damage. All these storms occurred in an environment with moderate MUCAPE, moderate to strong effective bulk shear, and primarily moderate DCAPE.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed onto two homes near the intersection of West 39th Street and Melrose Avenue in Erie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down Washington Township, Snyder County in the early evening of September 8, 2021. The tornado tracked southeastward for around three quarters of a mile, and had a maximum path width of around 30 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down along Route 104 just north of Parkwoods Road in Washington Township, Snyder County in the early evening of September 8, 2021. The tornado tracked southeastward for around three quarters of a mile, produced estimated maximum winds of around 95 mph, and had a maximum path width of around 30 yards, before lifting near Redcort Lane. Upwards of 100 trees were knocked down in a forested area near Redcort Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest Pennsylvania was within the warm sector during the morning of the 11th through early evening of the 13th as the region remained along the northwestern flank of the Bermuda-Azores subtropical high pressure ridge. Simultaneously, a very slow-moving cold front approached northwest Pennsylvania from the Upper Midwest as multiple surface lows moved northeastward along the front. Ahead of the front, three distinct rounds of severe thunderstorms with damaging wind gusts affected northwest Pennsylvania during the early evening hours of the 11th, 12th, and 13th, respectively. These storms utilized weak to strong MUCAPE, moderate to strong DCAPE, and primarily moderate effective bulk shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees downed in Conneaut Lake. One home was damaged and a mobile home was damaged severely.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Post Tropical Cyclone Ida brought heavy rain to eastern Pennsylvania on September 1.  Rainfall totals were as high as 5 to 10 inches.  The heavy rain caused significant flash flooding, mainly in the southeastern part of the state.  It resulted in widespread property damage.  There were several fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flash flooding occurred in Montgomery County with many road closures and vehicles stranded.  Numerous water rescues took place.  Many buildings and homes were flooded.  Most waterways in the county overflowed their banks, including the Schuylkill River, Perkiomen Creek and Wissahickon Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure was located over southern Lake Michigan during the early morning hours of December 11th. This low moved quickly northeast to north of Lake Ontario by mid-afternoon, deepening at a rate of almost 1 mb per hour. Ahead of this system, conditions were warm across the area with temperatures into the 60s in some locations during the early morning hours. Erie Airport hit a record high of 67 degrees. As this low pressure system moved east, it extended a cold front across Northwest Pennsylvania, which allowed for temperatures to crash significantly into the 30s later in the day. Behind the front, strong synoptic winds moved across the region with the pressure rises behind the system. Wind gusts as high as 64 mph were felt across the area with that highest report recorded by the automated weather sensor at Erie International Airport. Minor damage occurred across the region with downed trees and power lines and poles, which prompted numerous outages. Some of the trees landed upon vehicles causing property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on a car driving on Old French Road during the afternoon hours of the 11th. The driver was not injured. Winds gusted as high as 64 mph, which was recorded at Erie International Airport at 4:43 pm. Winds through the afternoon caused numerous trees to fall across the area including Millcreek Township, Girard Township, and Harborcreek. A large tree fell on a house near 4th and Pennsylvania in Erie. Two homes were damaged by trees on West 9th Street in Erie. At least 4 cars were hit by falling trees during the afternoon hours. In Harborcreek, a vehicle crashed into a fallen tree on Route 5, but no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper jet dynamics and deep moisture in the dendritic growth zone allowed for a short period of efficient snowfall, despite warm ground temperatures.  As the base of the upper trough pivoted across the region on the evening of November 14th, wind veered to the northwest, allowing scattered snow showers to continue into the morning of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tionesta recorded 2.5 inches of snow, while a location just west of Marienville received 1.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave trough crossed the Great Lakes during the evening of November 27th, with a corresponding surface low trailing behind it. Light snow overspread the region through the evening hours. A second trough arrived around dawn on the 28th, pushing the light snow north of the area.  Little upper level support and only a limited period of favorable snow growth tended to limit accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of accumulation was reported in the county, with the high value of 3.5 inches recorded by an NWS employee at Freeport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system produced a swath of light snow across approximately the northern half of central Pennsylvania from the late evening hours of Thursday, December 23 into the early morning hours of Friday, December 24, 2021. Snowfall was only a dusting to 2 inches, but it was enough to slicken roads and cause a couple of major road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Alberta Clipper produced around an inch of light snow and slickened roads across southern Lycoming County. Resultant slick roads and multiple accidents caused Route 15 to be closed in both directions over the mountain between Main Street in South Williamsport and Route 54 in Clinton Township. No serious injuries were reported, and the road was closed for just under two hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Alberta Clipper produced around a half an inch of light snow across southern Centre County, but it was enough to cause a closure of Route 322 in both directions near Route 45 due to icy conditions. The road was closed for a little more than two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from the Ohio Valley into the eastern Great Lakes on Monday, December 27, 2021, producing 1 to 2 inches of snow, before changing over to a wintry mix and eventually plain rain from southwest to northeast across the area. A burst of moderate snow at the onset of the precipitation quickly slickened roads, and I-80 westbound was closed at mile marker 140 in Rush Township, Centre County due to multiple accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A burst of moderate snow produced a quick 1 to 2 inches of snow that quickly slickened roads, and I-80 westbound was closed at mile marker 140 in Rush Township, Centre County due to multiple accidents. The westbound lanes were closed for nearly three and a half hours.

These storms utilized weak to strong MUCAPE, moderate to strong DCAPE, and primarily moderate effective bulk shear.

The tornado had maximum winds near 65 mph and a maximum path width of 10 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of scattered thunderstorms developed in a low shear, moderate CAPE, high DCAPE environment. The first round developed as scattered convection in southwest PA on the previous night and crossed into central PA by 11 AM EDT on Aug 10. Convection took on a linear mode with bowing characteristics as it approached Dauphin County by 2 PM EDT and ultimately produced damaging winds. Additional rounds of convection developed upstream later in the afternoon and again took on linear and bowing characteristics as it approached the Lower Susquehanna Valley by the evening hours. This line of storms converged with another line of storms moving northeastward from Virginia and Maryland. Although wind shear was weak, DCAPE values of near 1000 J/kg and storm motion near 15 kts allowed for damaging straight line wind to develop. There were no reports of hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing wind estimated near 60 mph downed trees on Stock St and on McCosh St in Hanover, PA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado touched down near Kreamer during the late afternoon of August 18, 2021. The tornado had estimated peak winds near 70 mph, a path length of around a half a mile, and a maximum path width of 15 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado touched down near Kreamer during the late afternoon of August 18, 2021. The tornado touched down near the Middleburg Livestock Auction barn along Route 522 and tacked northward for approximately half a mile before lifting near the intersection of Globe Mills Road and West Hollow Road. A tree fell onto a fence on the wet side of the barn and a horse trailer was flipped on its side. A convergence wind signature was also found in a cornfield, and some trees were damaged near the end of the tornado path. The tornado had maximum winds near 70 mph, a path length of around a half a mile and a maximum path width of 15 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down east of Halifax during the early evening of August 18, 2021. The tornado had estimated peak winds near 105 mph, a path length of around 5.5 miles, and a maximum path width of 150 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began west of the intersection of Parmer Drive and Price Road in Halifax Township, where it tore off a few foot section of the roof of a barn. The path then continued north to northeast, crossing Powells Valley Road. A row of trees was knocked down in a circular (counterclockwise) pattern, with a densely populated grove of trees being snapped and uprooted in varying directions to the east of that location. This is where the EF-1 damage began, with an estimated peak wind speed of 105 mph. Farther northeast and north of Hoover Lane, another grove of trees was snapped and knocked down in numerous directions. This was the end of the EF-1 caliber damage, with the tornado continuing to track northeast producing EF-0 intensity damage. Just north of Ridge Road and west of Hershey Road, a wheat field had several individual plants flattened. Just north of that wheat field, the tornado briefly weakened. The tornado then reintensified as it moved across Millwood Drive, where several trees were uprooted.  The tornado then lifted as it moved north of Millwood Drive.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado touched down near Winterstown during the evening of August 18, 2021. The tornado had estimated peak winds near 85 mph, a path length of around 0.7 miles, and a maximum path width of 125 yards. The tornado touched down south of List Road and tracked northeastward across Main Street before lifting just south of Pleasant View Road in North Hopewell Township/Winterstown Borough. Damage consisted of one to two dozen snapped/uprooted trees (mostly softwood pines) with some tops sheared off. Some of the tree tops were lofted 25 to 45 yards. Two homes along the path received minor damage to siding, and a wooden swing set was damaged.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado touched down west of East Berlin during the afternoon of August 18, 2021. The tornado had estimated peak winds near 65 mph, a path length of around 0.6 miles, and a maximum path width of 75 yards. The tornado began near the Reading Township Municipal Building on Church Street, where a few trees were downed and several large branches were snapped. Debris was lying in a convergent pattern along the northeastward track. The tornado continued northeastward along or just west of Church Road. Some corn crop along Church Road was damaged, lying northwestward toward the tornado track.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening line of showers and storms pushed into central Pennsylvania from the west during the afternoon of August 11, 2021. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shield of moderate to heavy rainfall, associated with the remnants of tropical cyclone Ida, moved from the DelMarVa peninsula to New England. This rainfall on top of several weeks of abnormally wet weather led to areas of small stream and urban flash flooding in Northeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding eroded and closed Sibley Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnant low pressure center of Hurricane Ida passed across West Virginia and the Central Appalachian Mountains from late August 31st through September 1st.  The abundant moisture associated with the low intersected a frontal zone which was nearly stalled in the area of the Ohio Valley.  Strong isentropic and frontogenetically-induced lift led to periods of heavy rain over much of southwestern Pennsylvania over the two-day period, both from thunderstorms ahead of the low and from a period of tropical rainfall during the morning of the 1st.  Rain tapered off by the afternoon as the remnants of Ida pulled towards the Mid-Atlantic coast.||The heaviest rainfall over the two-day period was observed in Westmoreland, Allegheny, and Washington Counties, with totals of 3 to 5 inches common. Lesser totals were noted to the north and south of this area, with areas near Interstate 80 mainly receiving a half-inch or less. The heavy rainfall resulted in widespread flooding of roads and streams, with several instances of evacuations noted.  Particularly hard hit were the municipalities of Millvale, Bridgeville, and Scott, all of which issued disaster declarations.  In Stowe Township, a school bus was trapped in flood waters, requiring the rescue of two adults and seven children.  Other rescues of trapped motorists were noted. Twenty-four residents had to be evacuated from an assisted living facility in Oakdale, where water up to 18 inches deep was observed across the town. ||Despite the heavy rain and numerous instances of flash and areal flooding, widespread river flooding was not observed after the rainfall ceased.  A dry slot near the Mason-Dixon Line on the morning of the 1st kept rainfall totals lower on the headwaters of the Monongahela River, allowing area reservoirs to mitigate the impact of the tropical rain downstream.  Thus, while short-term flooding was significant over a portion of the region, the overall event did not have the same impact as Gordon in 2018 or Frances/Ivan in 2014.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Alexandria Road was flooded.

The abundant moisture associated with the low intersected a frontal zone which was nearly stalled in the area of the Ohio Valley.  Strong isentropic and frontogenetically-induced lift led to periods of heavy rain over much of southwestern Pennsylvania over the two-day period, both from thunderstorms ahead of the low and from a period of tropical rainfall during the morning of the 1st.  Rain tapered off by the afternoon as the remnants of Ida pulled towards the Mid-Atlantic coast.||The heaviest rainfall over the two-day period was observed in Westmoreland, Allegheny, and Washington Counties, with totals of 3 to 5 inches common. Lesser totals were noted to the north and south of this area, with areas near Interstate 80 mainly receiving a half-inch or less. The heavy rainfall resulted in widespread flooding of roads and streams, with several instances of evacuations noted.  Particularly hard hit were the municipalities of Millvale, Bridgeville, and Scott, all of which issued disaster declarations.  In Stowe Township, a school bus was trapped in flood waters, requiring the rescue of two adults and seven children.  Other rescues of trapped motorists were noted. Twenty-four residents had to be evacuated from an assisted living facility in Oakdale, where water up to 18 inches deep was observed across the town. ||Despite the heavy rain and numerous instances of flash and areal flooding, widespread river flooding was not observed after the rainfall ceased.  A dry slot near the Mason-Dixon Line on the morning of the 1st kept rainfall totals lower on the headwaters of the Monongahela River, allowing area reservoirs to mitigate the impact of the tropical rain downstream.  Thus, while short-term flooding was significant over a portion of the region, the overall event did not have the same impact as Gordon in 2018 or Frances/Ivan in 2014.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on South Carbon Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and storms impacted northwestern Pennsylvania, developing in a weak low-level warm advection regime ahead of a shortwave trough over the Great Lakes. One of these cells was able to produce wind damage in Clearfield County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in multiple locations between Grampian and Glen Richey.
